input.new_string:
/-! ## §2. Skyscraper sheaves are flasque -/

/-! ### Project-local Mathlib supplement — Inner iso `skyscraperSheaf ≅ constantSheaf` on `PUnit`

The three definitions below (`alphaConstToSkyPUnit`, `betaSkyToConstPUnit`,
`alpha_beta_eq_toSheafify`) form the substrate of the **inner iso** for
`Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const`. On the one-point space
`PUnit`, the constant sheaf at `A` agrees with the skyscraper sheaf at
`PUnit.unit` with value `A`: both have value `A` at the open `⊤` (since
`PUnit.unit ∈ ⊤`) and terminal value at the open `⊥` (by the sheaf
condition on the empty open).

The forward and inverse maps are built directly:
- `alphaConstToSkyPUnit` : `(Functor.const _).obj A → skyscraperPresheaf PUnit.unit A`
  — the obvious presheaf morphism: `eqToHom` at non-empty opens, terminal
  map at `⊥`.
- `betaSkyToConstPUnit` : `skyscraperPresheaf PUnit.unit A → ((constantSheaf J).obj A).val`
  — the inverse: `eqToHom ≫ toSheafify` at non-empty opens, terminal
  map at `⊥`.
- `alpha_beta_eq_toSheafify` : the composition collapses to the
  sheafification unit, the key identity that makes both iso directions
  follow via `toSheafify_sheafifyLift` and `sheafify_hom_ext`.

Iter-197 Lane H closure: replaces the iter-196 `sorry` at the inner iso
of `skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const`. The full iso is consumed
inline in that theorem below (see Step 2). -/

/-- Presheaf morphism from the constant presheaf at `A` to the
skyscraper presheaf at `PUnit.unit` with value `A`. Used as input to
`sheafifyLift` to build the forward direction of the inner iso. -/
noncomputable def alphaConstToSkyPUnit
    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar]
    [∀ U : TopologicalSpace.Opens (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}),
      Decidable (PUnit.unit ∈ U)]
    (A : ModuleCat.{u} kbar) :
    (CategoryTheory.Functor.const
        ((TopologicalSpace.Opens (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}))ᵒᵖ)).obj A ⟶
      skyscraperPresheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar) PUnit.unit A where
  app U := if h : PUnit.unit ∈ U.unop then eqToHom (by simp [skyscraperPresheaf, h])
    else ((if_neg h).symm.ndrec terminalIsTerminal).from _
  naturality {U V} i := by
    simp only [CategoryTheory.Functor.const_obj_obj, skyscraperPresheaf_obj,
      CategoryTheory.Functor.const_obj_map, Category.id_comp]
    by_cases hV : PUnit.unit ∈ V.unop
    · have hU : PUnit.unit ∈ U.unop := i.unop.le hV
      simp only [skyscraperPresheaf_map, dif_pos hV, dif_pos hU, eqToHom_trans]
    · apply ((if_neg hV).symm.ndrec terminalIsTerminal).hom_ext

/-- Presheaf morphism from the skyscraper presheaf at `PUnit.unit` with
value `A` to the underlying presheaf of the constant sheaf at `A` on
`PUnit`. The inverse of `alphaConstToSkyPUnit` modulo sheafification. -/
noncomputable def betaSkyToConstPUnit
    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar]
    [∀ U : TopologicalSpace.Opens (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}),
      Decidable (PUnit.unit ∈ U)]
    (A : ModuleCat.{u} kbar) :
    skyscraperPresheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar) PUnit.unit A ⟶
      ((constantSheaf (TopologicalSpace.Opens.grothendieckTopology
            (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}))
          (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj A).val := by
  let J := TopologicalSpace.Opens.grothendieckTopology (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1})
  let D := ModuleCat.{u} kbar
  let CS := (constantSheaf J D).obj A
  refine
    { app := fun U => ?_
      naturality := fun {U V} i => ?_ }
  · by_cases h : PUnit.unit ∈ U.unop
    · exact eqToHom (by simp [skyscraperPresheaf, h]) ≫
        (toSheafify J ((CategoryTheory.Functor.const
            ((TopologicalSpace.Opens (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}))ᵒᵖ)).obj A)).app U
    · have hU : U.unop = ⊥ := by
        rcases U.unop.eq_bot_or_top with hbot | htop
        · exact hbot
        · exfalso; apply h; rw [htop]; trivial
      exact (TopCat.Sheaf.isTerminalOfEqEmpty CS hU).from _
  · by_cases hV : PUnit.unit ∈ V.unop
    case neg =>
      have hVbot : V.unop = ⊥ := by
        rcases V.unop.eq_bot_or_top with hbot | htop
        · exact hbot
        · exfalso; apply hV; rw [htop]; trivial
      exact (TopCat.Sheaf.isTerminalOfEqEmpty CS hVbot).hom_ext _ _
    case pos =>
      have hU : PUnit.unit ∈ U.unop := i.unop.le hV
      simp only [dif_pos hV, dif_pos hU, skyscraperPresheaf_map]
      have hnat := (toSheafify J
        ((CategoryTheory.Functor.const
            ((TopologicalSpace.Opens (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}))ᵒᵖ)).obj A)).naturality i
      simp only [CategoryTheory.Functor.const_obj_obj,
        CategoryTheory.Functor.const_obj_map, Category.id_comp] at hnat
      rw [hnat]
      simp [eqToHom_trans_assoc]
      rfl

/-- Composition lemma: `alphaConstToSkyPUnit ≫ betaSkyToConstPUnit` equals
the sheafification unit. This is the key identity that makes both
directions of the inner iso commute. -/
lemma alphaConstToSkyPUnit_comp_betaSkyToConstPUnit_eq_toSheafify
    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar]
    [∀ U : TopologicalSpace.Opens (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}),
      Decidable (PUnit.unit ∈ U)]
    (A : ModuleCat.{u} kbar) :
    alphaConstToSkyPUnit A ≫ betaSkyToConstPUnit A =
      toSheafify (TopologicalSpace.Opens.grothendieckTopology
          (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}))
        ((CategoryTheory.Functor.const
            ((TopologicalSpace.Opens (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}))ᵒᵖ)).obj A) := by
  apply CategoryTheory.NatTrans.ext
  funext U
  by_cases h : PUnit.unit ∈ U.unop
  · change (alphaConstToSkyPUnit A).app U ≫ (betaSkyToConstPUnit A).app U = _
    simp only [alphaConstToSkyPUnit, betaSkyToConstPUnit, dif_pos h]
    simp [eqToHom_trans_assoc]
  · have hU : U.unop = ⊥ := by
      rcases U.unop.eq_bot_or_top with hbot | htop
      · exact hbot
      · exfalso; apply h; rw [htop]; trivial
    apply (TopCat.Sheaf.isTerminalOfEqEmpty
      ((constantSheaf (TopologicalSpace.Opens.grothendieckTopology
          (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}))
        (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj A) hU).hom_ext

/-- **Inner iso**: on the one-point space `PUnit`, the skyscraper sheaf
at `PUnit.unit` with value `A` is isomorphic to the constant sheaf at
`A`. This closes the inner-iso gap of
`Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const` below. -/
noncomputable def Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_iso_constantSheaf_punit
    (kbar : Type u) [Field kbar]
    [∀ U : TopologicalSpace.Opens (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}),
      Decidable (PUnit.unit ∈ U)]
    (A : ModuleCat.{u} kbar) :
    skyscraperSheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar)
        (X := TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}) (PUnit.unit : PUnit.{u + 1}) A ≅
      (constantSheaf (TopologicalSpace.Opens.grothendieckTopology
          (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}))
        (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj A := by
  refine
    { hom := ⟨betaSkyToConstPUnit A⟩
      inv := ⟨sheafifyLift _ (alphaConstToSkyPUnit A)
        (skyscraperPresheaf_isSheaf _ _)⟩
      hom_inv_id := ?_
      inv_hom_id := ?_ }
  · apply CategoryTheory.Sheaf.hom_ext
    have key : betaSkyToConstPUnit A ≫ sheafifyLift
        (TopologicalSpace.Opens.grothendieckTopology (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}))
        (alphaConstToSkyPUnit A) (skyscraperPresheaf_isSheaf _ _) = 𝟙 _ := by
      apply CategoryTheory.NatTrans.ext
      funext U
      by_cases h : PUnit.unit ∈ U.unop
      · change (betaSkyToConstPUnit A).app U ≫
          (sheafifyLift _ (alphaConstToSkyPUnit A) _).app U = _
        have htsl := toSheafify_sheafifyLift
          (TopologicalSpace.Opens.grothendieckTopology (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}))
          (alphaConstToSkyPUnit A) (skyscraperPresheaf_isSheaf _ _)
        simp only [betaSkyToConstPUnit, dif_pos h]
        change (eqToHom _ ≫ (toSheafify
            (TopologicalSpace.Opens.grothendieckTopology (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}))
            _).app U) ≫
          (sheafifyLift (TopologicalSpace.Opens.grothendieckTopology
              (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}))
            (alphaConstToSkyPUnit A) (skyscraperPresheaf_isSheaf _ _)).app U = _
        rw [Category.assoc]
        rw [show (toSheafify
              (TopologicalSpace.Opens.grothendieckTopology
                (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}))
              ((CategoryTheory.Functor.const
                  ((TopologicalSpace.Opens (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}))ᵒᵖ)).obj A)).app U ≫
            (sheafifyLift (TopologicalSpace.Opens.grothendieckTopology
                (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}))
              (alphaConstToSkyPUnit A) (skyscraperPresheaf_isSheaf _ _)).app U =
            (alphaConstToSkyPUnit A).app U
            from congrFun (congrArg CategoryTheory.NatTrans.app htsl) U]
        simp only [alphaConstToSkyPUnit, dif_pos h, eqToHom_trans, eqToHom_refl]
        rfl
      · have hU : U.unop = ⊥ := by
          rcases U.unop.eq_bot_or_top with hbot | htop
          · exact hbot
          · exfalso; apply h; rw [htop]; trivial
        apply (TopCat.Sheaf.isTerminalOfEqEmpty
          (skyscraperSheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar)
            (X := TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}) (PUnit.unit : PUnit.{u + 1}) A) hU).hom_ext
    exact key
  · apply CategoryTheory.Sheaf.hom_ext
    change sheafifyLift _ (alphaConstToSkyPUnit A) _ ≫ betaSkyToConstPUnit A = 𝟙 _
    apply sheafify_hom_ext _ _ _
      ((constantSheaf (TopologicalSpace.Opens.grothendieckTopology
          (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}))
        (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj A).property
    change toSheafify _ _ ≫ sheafifyLift _ (alphaConstToSkyPUnit A) _ ≫
        betaSkyToConstPUnit A = toSheafify _ _ ≫ 𝟙 _
    rw [← Category.assoc, toSheafify_sheafifyLift]
    change alphaConstToSkyPUnit A ≫ betaSkyToConstPUnit A = toSheafify
      (TopologicalSpace.Opens.grothendieckTopology (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}))
      ((CategoryTheory.Functor.const
          ((TopologicalSpace.Opens (TopCat.of PUnit.{u + 1}))ᵒᵖ)).obj A) ≫ 𝟙 _
    rw [Category.comp_id]
    exact alphaConstToSkyPUnit_comp_betaSkyToConstPUnit_eq_toSheafify A

/-- **Skyscraper sheaf as pushforward of a constant sheaf** (Hartshorne
II.1, Exercise 1.17).

# AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/H1Vanishing.lean

## Session summary

- **Lane H HARD BAR MET via Route H-2.** 1 sorry closed (3 → 2) on the
  inner iso path of `Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const` (L851
  in original file). The closure introduces 4 new axiom-clean
  project-local declarations + replaces the inner-iso `sorry` with a
  direct construction.
- Built axiom-clean: `alphaConstToSkyPUnit` (L831),
  `betaSkyToConstPUnit` (L859),
  `alphaConstToSkyPUnit_comp_betaSkyToConstPUnit_eq_toSheafify` (L905),
  `Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_iso_constantSheaf_punit` (L924).
- Both `Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_iso_constantSheaf_punit` and
  `Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const` verified
  axiom-clean via `lean_verify`: only `{propext, Classical.choice,
  Quot.sound}`.
- Build GREEN, sorry count 3 → 2 in this file (file-local), no axiom
  regression.

## Summary

- **Declarations added (axiom-clean)**: 4
  - `alphaConstToSkyPUnit` (presheaf morphism `(Functor.const).obj A → skyscraperPresheaf PUnit.unit A`)
  - `betaSkyToConstPUnit` (presheaf morphism `skyscraperPresheaf PUnit.unit A → ((constantSheaf).obj A).val`)
  - `alphaConstToSkyPUnit_comp_betaSkyToConstPUnit_eq_toSheafify` (composition lemma)
  - `Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_iso_constantSheaf_punit` (the inner iso)
- **Sorries closed**: 1 (the `hinner_iso` sorry at original L851/855)
- **Sorries remaining in file**: 2 (L138 `IsFlasque.constant_of_irreducible`
  non-empty branch; L613 `IsFlasque.injective_flasque` j_! gap)
- **Sorry count delta**: 3 → 2 (−1) in this file.

## alphaConstToSkyPUnit (L831)
- **Approach:** Define a natural transformation pointwise:
  at op U with `PUnit.unit ∈ U`, use `eqToHom` (both sides equal `A`);
  at op U with `PUnit.unit ∉ U`, use `IsTerminal.from` to the terminal
  in `ModuleCat kbar`.
- **Result:** RESOLVED — axiom-clean.

## betaSkyToConstPUnit (L859)
- **Approach:** Define pointwise:
  at op U with `PUnit.unit ∈ U`, compose `eqToHom` (to lift
  `skyscraperPresheaf.obj` to `A`) with `(toSheafify).app U` (the
  sheafification unit at op U).
  at op U with `PUnit.unit ∉ U`, observe `U.unop = ⊥` (using
  `Opens.eq_bot_or_top` for `IndiscreteTopology` of `PUnit`), then both
  sides are terminal — use `TopCat.Sheaf.isTerminalOfEqEmpty` + `from`.
  Naturality on the non-trivial restriction is via `toSheafify`'s own
  naturality.
- **Result:** RESOLVED — axiom-clean.

## alphaConstToSkyPUnit_comp_betaSkyToConstPUnit_eq_toSheafify (L905)
- **Approach:** Per-open case analysis. `PUnit.unit ∈ U.unop` case
  reduces to `eqToHom_trans_assoc`; the `⊥` case is closed via
  `IsTerminal.hom_ext` since both sides land in a terminal.
- **Result:** RESOLVED — axiom-clean.

## Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_iso_constantSheaf_punit (L924)
- **Approach:** Iso built as a `{ hom, inv, hom_inv_id, inv_hom_id }`
  record. `hom = ⟨betaSkyToConstPUnit A⟩`; `inv = ⟨sheafifyLift α⟩`.
  `inv_hom_id` (i.e. `α-lift ≫ β = 𝟙` on `(constantSheaf).obj A`) via
  `sheafify_hom_ext` + the composition identity.
  `hom_inv_id` (i.e. `β ≫ α-lift = 𝟙` on `skyscraperSheaf`) via
  `toSheafify_sheafifyLift` for the `PUnit.unit ∈ U` branch and
  `TopCat.Sheaf.isTerminalOfEqEmpty.hom_ext` for the `⊥` branch.
- **Result:** RESOLVED — axiom-clean. The `change` tactic is used

Active prover mode: **mathlib-build**

## Your goal

Build project-local Mathlib infrastructure axiom-clean, one step at a time, going **as far as possible** in this iteration. Your output consists of definitions and lemmas that compile without `sorry` and whose `#print axioms` shows only `{propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}`. You stop only when you hit a genuine mathematical blocker — not because one step is hard.

## Invariant

**No `sorry` in your output.** Every declaration you add must be fully proved. If you cannot close the current step: try alternatives first, then either prove a smaller sub-step or leave the declaration absent. Never add a declaration with `sorry`.

## Workflow

### 1. Orient

- Read `PROGRESS.md` for the objective: what infrastructure is needed, and why.
- Read the relevant blueprint chapter. It often contains the mathematical path to the ingredient you're building.
- Read `task_pending.md` for prior dead ends. Do not repeat them.

### 2. Scout the Mathlib API

Before writing any Lean, search for what already exists:

- `lean_local_search` for related names.
- `lean_leansearch` with a description of the mathematical content.
- `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns.
- Look for near-misses: a lemma that covers a sub-case, an instance you can compose, a `simp` set that contains what you need.

Even if you think you know the API — search anyway. Signatures change, instances disappear.

### 3. Build bottom-up, step by step

Start from the deepest missing piece identified in your scouting. For each step:

1. **Draft the type signature** and check it typechecks via `lean_diagnostic_messages` or `lean_run_code` before attempting the body.
2. **Write the proof.** Use Mathlib lemmas you verified exist. If a sub-step is missing, recurse: prove that sub-step first.
3. **Verify axiom-cleanliness** immediately after the step compiles: `#print axioms MyLemma`. Any `sorryAx` in the ancestry means the step is not axiom-clean — do not proceed until it is gone.
4. **Continue to the next step** in the chain.

Push as far as you can. Each axiom-clean step you add is permanent progress.

### 4. When stuck on a step — try before stopping

Before declaring a step impossible:

1. **Try a different proof route.** Reformulate the statement, weaken to a special case you can prove, use a detour through an equivalent form.
2. **Use the informal agent** if an API key is available (check `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` first): `.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py` "Prove [goal] using only current Mathlib." Formalize whatever it suggests. If no key is set, skip to step 3.
3. **Search more broadly.** The lemma you need might exist under a different name or in a namespace you haven't checked.
4. **Prove a strictly smaller sub-step** that is axiom-clean and genuinely useful — something that shrinks the remaining gap.

Only after exhausting these alternatives do you stop. **Comments describing an approach you haven't tried are not exhausted alternatives — attempt them first.**

### 5. Stopping

**Before stopping, run this self-review:**

1. Did I attempt every approach I wrote in a comment, TODO, or log entry? If not, go back and attempt them.
2. Are there adjacent declarations in the chain I could attempt with the infrastructure I just built?
3. Did I call the informal agent (or document that no key was available) before declaring the step impossible?
4. Is there any approach I thought of but skipped? Attempt it — a failed attempt with a named error is better than a comment.

Stop only when all four answers are "yes" or "not applicable." **Writing a comment about a possible approach counts as identifying it — which means you should attempt it.**

**You are fully authorized to make every mathematical and structural decision within your assigned file without waiting for the planner.** "I'll let the planner decide which route to take" is never a valid stop reason — pick the most promising route, attempt it, document the outcome. The planner corrects route choices next iter; an idle session corrects nothing.

Stop when you have tried alternatives and cannot make further axiom-clean progress. Before stopping:

- Commit all axiom-clean steps you completed.
- Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with a precise handoff (see Logging below).
- Ensure the file compiles with no `sorry`.

## File structure

Place new declarations under a clearly delimited section:

```lean
/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — <TopicName> -/
```

- Use `private` for helpers with no downstream use outside this file.
- Use `theorem` / `lemma` (non-private) for steps that other files will import.
- Add a one-line docstring to each non-private declaration explaining why it is project-local.

## Protected declarations

Read `archon-protected.yaml` before touching any existing declaration. You may add new declarations freely. You must not rename, re-type, or modify signatures of protected declarations.

## API alignment

Mathlib names and signatures are the most common failure source.

- Verify every name with `lean_local_search` or `lean_loogle` — do not rely on memory.
- Check that typeclass assumptions in your statement match what Mathlib requires.
- For `def`-backed types: Lean may not unfold them for typeclass synthesis; use explicit coercions or `change` / `show` in the proof body.

## Write permissions

Only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.

## Logging

Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror the `.lean` path):

```markdown
# Algebra/MathlibSupplement.lean

## Session summary
- Built axiom-clean: `Foo.bar`, `Foo.baz` (lines 12–40)
- Blocked on: `Foo.qux` — needs `Bar.aux` which requires [precise statement]

## Foo.bar (line 12)
- **Approach:** Direct from `Mathlib.Algebra.X` via `simp [Y]`
- **Result:** RESOLVED — axiom-clean

## Foo.qux (not added)
- **Approach 1:** Tried [route] — FAILED because [reason]
- **Approach 2:** Tried informal agent — suggested [sketch], formalization blocked on [specific gap]
- **Next step:** Build `Bar.aux : [precise type statement]` first
- **Dead end:** Do not retry [route] — [why it fails]
```

One section per declaration attempted. For each: approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / NOT ADDED), dead-end warnings, next step.

## End-of-session handoff

**Write a `## Summary` section** before the verdicts: declarations added (count + names), declarations blocked (count + why), sorry count before → after across your file.

**Write a `## Why I stopped` section** — be brutally honest. Only axiom-clean Lean declarations count as progress. Comments, sketches, and log prose do NOT. If you wrote down approaches you didn't attempt, say so:

- `Real progress`: N axiom-clean declarations added — name each one with its line number.
- `Partial progress`: added some declarations, hit a specific blocker on the next — name the blocker precisely (not "it's complex", but the exact type or missing ingredient).
- `Approaches written but not attempted`: identified routes in comments or log but did not attempt them — name each. This is valid but weak; the planner will re-assign with a directive to attempt them.
- `Blocked — alternatives exhausted`: informal agent called (or key unavailable, documented), alternative routes tried, all failed — name what was tried, why each failed, and the exact type statement of the next needed ingredient.
- `Infrastructure already exists`: Mathlib has what was needed — cite the exact lemma name and namespace.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

11	# The line bundle `𝒪_C(P)` of a closed point and its global sections (RR.3)
12	
13	This file is the **RR.3** sub-build chapter for the project's headline
14	`genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (the "smooth proper geometrically irreducible
15	genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is isomorphic to `ℙ¹`" lemma in
16	`AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity`).
17	
18	The Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 chain for the genus-`0` ↦ `ℙ¹` classification routes
19	through:
20	
21	- `RR.1` (`WeilDivisor.lean`): the Weil divisor group `Div(C)` and the
22	  degree map `deg : Div(C) → ℤ`.
23	- `RR.2` (`RRFormula.lean`): the Euler-characteristic identity
24	  `χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g` and the Riemann–Roch dimension formula
25	  `ℓ(D) = deg(D) + 1` in genus `0`.
26	- **`RR.3` (this file)**: the invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(P)` of a closed point
27	  `P ∈ C`, its `k̄`-vector space of global sections as the
28	  Riemann–Roch space `L([P])`, the `H¹`-vanishing
29	  `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 0` on a genus-`0` curve, the dimension formula
30	  `dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 2`, and the existence of a non-constant
31	  rational function `f ∈ K(C)` with at most a simple pole at `P`.
32	- `RR.4` (`RationalCurveIso.lean`, future): the "two-section
33	  ⇒ `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections` ⇒ `≅ ℙ¹`" classification.
34

56	The 5 pinned declarations are:

58	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint` — the invertible
59	   sheaf `𝒪_C(P)` associated to a closed point `P` on a smooth proper
60	   curve `C / k̄`.
61	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.globalSections_iff`
62	   — the identification of global sections of `𝒪_C(P)` with the
63	   Riemann–Roch space
64	   `L([P]) = {f ∈ K(C)^× | div(f) + [P] ≥ 0} ∪ {0}`,
65	   expressed as an `Iff`-style characterisation of the order conditions
66	   `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for `Q ≠ P` and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1`.
67	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.h1_vanishing_genusZero`
68	   — the cohomological vanishing `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 0` on a smooth proper
69	   geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄` with `g(C) = 0`, via the long
70	   exact sequence of the closed-point short exact sequence
71	   `0 → 𝒪_C → 𝒪_C(P) → k(P) → 0`.
72	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.dim_eq_two_of_genusZero`
73	   — the dimension formula `dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 2` in genus `0`,
74	   specialising the χ-identity `RR.2` to `D = [P]` and consuming the
75	   `H¹`-vanishing of pin 3.

77	   — the corollary: a non-constant rational function `f ∈ K(C)` regular
78	   on `C \ {P}` with at most a simple pole at `P`, obtained as a lift of
79	   any non-zero element of the quotient

110	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
111	
112	/-! ## §1. The line bundle of a closed point on a smooth proper curve
113	
114	For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄` and a closed
115	point `P ∈ C`, the local ring `𝒪_{C,P}` is a DVR with maximal ideal
116	generated by a uniformiser `f_P`. Hartshorne's construction `ℒ(D)`
117	(II §6 p. 144) applied to the Cartier divisor `[P]` (locally cut out by
118	`f_P` near `P`, by `1` elsewhere) produces the invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(P)`:
119	the sub-`𝒪_C`-module of the function-field constant sheaf `𝒦_C ≅ K(C)`
120	generated locally by `f_P^{-1}` near `P` and by `1` on `C \ {P}`. -/
121	
122	namespace Scheme
123	
124	/-! ### Hartshorne subsheaf-of-`K_C` carrier (iter-183 Lane A scaffold)
125	
126	The substantive iter-183 contribution: a concrete per-open `Set`-valued
127	carrier of the line bundle `𝒪_C(P)`, realised directly as Hartshorne's
128	subsheaf of the function-field constant sheaf `K_C` (Hartshorne II §6
129	p. 144; analogist `ocofp-sheaf-internalhom.md` Decision 3
130	`ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB`).
131	
132	A section of `𝒪_C(P)` over an open `U` is a rational function `f ∈ K(C)`
133	satisfying the order conditions `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor
134	`Q ≠ P` with `Q.point ∈ U` (regularity on the complement of `P` inside
135	`U`) and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1` when `P ∈ U` (at most a simple pole at `P`).
136	The construction is independent of the choice of uniformiser at `P`:
137	any two uniformisers differ by a unit, so the order-`≥ −1` condition at
138	`P` is intrinsic.
139	
140	iter-184+ will upgrade `carrierSet` to a `Submodule kbar K(C)` via the
141	closure proofs (zero / addition / kbar-scalar), bundle the result as a
142	presheaf functor (identity-on-`K(C)` restrictions, monotone in `U` via
143	`carrierSet_mono`), and discharge the sheaf property via gluing-by-
144	stalks (stalk-locality of the order conditions at each prime divisor). -/
145	
146	/-- **Carrier set** of `𝒪_C(P)` over an open `U : (Opens C.left)ᵒᵖ`: the
147	set of rational functions `f ∈ K(C)` satisfying the order conditions
148	`ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor `Q` with `Q.point ∈ U.unop`,
149	`Q.point ≠ P`, and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1` when `P ∈ U.unop`.
150	
151	iter-183 Lane A landed this as a concrete substantive `Set` definition;
152	no sorry. -/
153	private noncomputable def lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet
154	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
155	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsIntegral C.left]
156	    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
157	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
158	    (P : C.left) (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1)
159	    (U : (TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)ᵒᵖ) :
160	    Set C.left.functionField := by
161	  let Phat : C.left.PrimeDivisor := ⟨P, hPcoh⟩
162	  haveI := Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instKrullDimLEStalk
163	    (X := C.left) Phat
164	  exact { f | (∀ Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor, Q.point ∈ U.unop.1 → Q.point ≠ P →
165	              0 ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q f) ∧
166	              (P ∈ U.unop.1 → (-1 : ℤ) ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Phat f) }
167	
168	/-- **Monotonicity of `carrierSet` in `U`**: when `V.unop ⊆ U.unop` (i.e.
169	the open `V` is contained in the open `U`), the carrier on `U` is
170	INCLUDED in the carrier on `V` (the order conditions over the smaller
171	open `V` involve fewer prime divisors, hence are easier to satisfy).
172	
173	This is the substantive monotonicity that drives the (identity-on-`K(C)`)
174	restriction map of the would-be `lineBundleAtClosedPoint.presheaf` functor:
175	in `(Opens C.left)ᵒᵖ`, an arrow `U ⟶ V` corresponds to `V.unop ⊆ U.unop`,
176	and the restriction map `carrierSet U → carrierSet V` is the inclusion
177	delivered by this lemma.
178	
179	iter-183 Lane A landed this as the substantive monotonicity proof; no
180	sorry. -/
181	private lemma lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet_mono
182	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
183	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsIntegral C.left]
184	    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
185	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
186	    (P : C.left) (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1)
187	    {U V : (TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)ᵒᵖ}
188	    (hUV : V.unop.1 ⊆ U.unop.1) :
189	    lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet P hPcoh U
190	      ⊆ lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet P hPcoh V := by
191	  intro f hf
192	  refine ⟨fun Q hQV hQP => hf.1 Q (hUV hQV) hQP, fun hPV => hf.2 (hUV hPV)⟩
193	
194	/-- **Nonempty top-open**: for an integral scheme `X`, the top open is
195	nonempty (its carrier is `Set.univ` and `X` itself is nonempty since
196	`IsIntegral X ⟹ IrreducibleSpace X ⟹ Nonempty X`). Supplied as an
197	instance so that the standard Mathlib `Algebra Γ(X, U) X.functionField`
198	instance fires at `U = ⊤`. -/
199	instance lineBundleAtClosedPoint.instNonemptyTopOpen
200	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] :
201	    Nonempty ((⊤ : X.Opens) : Scheme) :=
202	  (Scheme.Opens.nonempty_iff (U := (⊤ : X.Opens))).mpr
203	    ⟨(inferInstance : Nonempty X).some, Set.mem_univ _⟩
204	
205	/-- **`kbar`-algebra structure on the function field `K(C)`**. The
206	project's `Scheme.toModuleKSheaf.algebraSection` instance gives
207	`Algebra kbar Γ(C.left, ⊤)`, and Mathlib's standard
208	`AlgebraicGeometry.instAlgebraCarrierObjOppositeOpens...` gives
209	`Algebra Γ(C.left, ⊤) K(C)` via `germToFunctionField`. Composing the
210	two algebra maps via `RingHom.toAlgebra` produces the desired
211	`Algebra kbar K(C)` instance, which is needed for `Submodule kbar K(C)`
212	to type-check. -/
213	noncomputable instance lineBundleAtClosedPoint.instAlgebraKbarFunctionField
214	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar]
215	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsIntegral C.left] :
216	    Algebra kbar C.left.functionField := by
217	  haveI : Nonempty (⊤ : C.left.Opens) :=
218	    (AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Opens.nonempty_iff
219	      (X := C.left) (U := (⊤ : C.left.Opens))).mpr
220	      ⟨(inferInstance : Nonempty C.left).some, Set.mem_univ _⟩
221	  exact RingHom.toAlgebra
222	    ((Scheme.germToFunctionField C.left (⊤ : C.left.Opens)).hom.comp
223	      (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf.kToSection C
224	        (Opposite.op (⊤ : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat))).hom)
225	
226	/-- **Carrier submodule** of `𝒪_C(P)` over an open `U`: upgrade of
227	`carrierSet U` to a `Submodule kbar K(C)` via the three closure proofs
228	(`0`, `+`, `kbar • _`). The closure proofs rest on the DVR-shipped
229	`Ring.ordFrac_add` (non-archimedean inequality on the discrete valuation
230	of a regular-in-codim-1 stalk; iter-186 Step 1 upgraded the carrier class
231	so this is invocable) and `Ring.ordFrac_of_isUnit` (the scalar from
232	`kbar` becomes a unit in the stalk, preserving the order under scalar
233	multiplication).
234	
235	iter-186 Step 2 (per analogist `ocofp-carrierset-submodule-api.md`
236	Decision 2): structural skeleton with the load-bearing Mathlib lemmas in
237	place; bookkeeping `sorry`s remain for the prover phase. -/
238	private noncomputable def lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSubmodule
239	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
240	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsIntegral C.left]
241	    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
242	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
243	    (P : C.left) (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1)
244	    (U : (TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)ᵒᵖ) :
245	    Submodule kbar C.left.functionField where
246	  carrier := lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSet P hPcoh U
247	  zero_mem' := by
248	    -- `order Y 0 = WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac _ 0) = WithZero.log 0 = 0`;
249	    -- both `0 ≤ 0` and `-1 ≤ 0` hold trivially.
250	    refine ⟨fun Q _ _ => ?_, fun _ => ?_⟩
251	    · simp [Scheme.RationalMap.order]
252	    · simp [Scheme.RationalMap.order]
253	  add_mem' := by
254	    -- Case-split on `f + g = 0` (trivial via `WithZero.log_zero`), else
255	    -- apply `Ring.ordFrac_add` (DVR-shipped, Step 1 unlocks) and
256	    -- `WithZero.log` monotonicity on the nonzero part.
257	    rintro a b ⟨ha₁, ha₂⟩ ⟨hb₁, hb₂⟩
258	    have hMNZ : ∀ (Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor) (x : C.left.functionField),
259	        x ≠ 0 → Ring.ordFrac (C.left.presheaf.stalk Q.point) x ≠ 0 := by
260	      intro Q x hx; simp [hx]
261	    have key : ∀ (Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor) (n : ℤ),
262	        n ≤ 0 → n ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q a →
263	          n ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q b →
264	          n ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q (a + b) := by
265	      intro Q n hn haₚ hbₚ
266	      by_cases hab : a + b = 0
267	      · simpa [hab, Scheme.RationalMap.order] using hn
268	      by_cases hae : a = 0
269	      · rw [hae, zero_add]; exact hbₚ
270	      by_cases hbe : b = 0
271	      · rw [hbe, add_zero]; exact haₚ
272	      set R := C.left.presheaf.stalk Q.point
273	      have hoa : Ring.ordFrac R a ≠ 0 := hMNZ Q a hae
274	      have hob : Ring.ordFrac R b ≠ 0 := hMNZ Q b hbe
275	      have hoab : Ring.ordFrac R (a + b) ≠ 0 := hMNZ Q _ hab
276	      have hmin : Ring.ordFrac R a ⊓ Ring.ordFrac R b ≤ Ring.ordFrac R (a + b) :=
277	        Ring.ordFrac_add (R := R) a b hab
278	      have hlog : (Ring.ordFrac R a ⊓ Ring.ordFrac R b).log ≤
279	          Scheme.RationalMap.order Q (a + b) := by
280	        rcases min_cases (Ring.ordFrac R a) (Ring.ordFrac R b) with
281	            ⟨heq, _⟩ | ⟨heq, _⟩
282	        · rw [heq]; exact (WithZero.log_le_log hoa hoab).mpr (heq ▸ hmin)
283	        · rw [heq]; exact (WithZero.log_le_log hob hoab).mpr (heq ▸ hmin)
284	      have hminbd : n ≤ (Ring.ordFrac R a ⊓ Ring.ordFrac R b).log := by
285	        rcases min_cases (Ring.ordFrac R a) (Ring.ordFrac R b) with
286	            ⟨heq, _⟩ | ⟨heq, _⟩
287	        · rw [heq]; exact haₚ
288	        · rw [heq]; exact hbₚ
289	      linarith
290	    refine ⟨fun Q hQU hQP => key Q 0 le_rfl (ha₁ Q hQU hQP) (hb₁ Q hQU hQP),
291	      fun hPU => key ⟨P, hPcoh⟩ (-1) (by norm_num) (ha₂ hPU) (hb₂ hPU)⟩
292	  smul_mem' := by
293	    -- For `c : kbar`, the scalar action `c • f` on `K(C)` factors through
294	    -- `algebraMap kbar K(C)`. For `c ≠ 0`, `algebraMap kbar K(C) c` lifts to
295	    -- a nonzero `β` in every stalk via the germ map; `Ring.ordFrac_ge_one_of_ne_zero`
296	    -- on `β` then gives `order Q (algebraMap c) ≥ 0`, hence `c•x ∈ carrierSet`.
297	    intro c x ⟨hx₁, hx₂⟩
298	    rcases eq_or_ne c 0 with rfl | hc
299	    · -- `c = 0` ⇒ `0 • x = 0` ∈ carrierSet via `zero_mem'`.
300	      simp only [zero_smul]
301	      refine ⟨fun Q _ _ => ?_, fun _ => ?_⟩ <;> simp [Scheme.RationalMap.order]
302	    rcases eq_or_ne x 0 with rfl | hx_ne
303	    · -- `x = 0` ⇒ `c • 0 = 0` ∈ carrierSet.
304	      simp only [smul_zero]
305	      refine ⟨fun Q _ _ => ?_, fun _ => ?_⟩ <;> simp [Scheme.RationalMap.order]
306	    -- Both `c ≠ 0` and `x ≠ 0`: compute `order Q (c•x)` via multiplicativity.
307	    have hsmul : c • x = (algebraMap kbar C.left.functionField c) * x :=
308	      Algebra.smul_def c x
309	    have hαne : (algebraMap kbar C.left.functionField c) ≠ 0 := by
310	      have hinj := FaithfulSMul.algebraMap_injective kbar C.left.functionField
311	      simpa using hinj.ne_iff.mpr hc
312	    have hMNZ : ∀ (Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor) (y : C.left.functionField),
313	        y ≠ 0 → Ring.ordFrac (C.left.presheaf.stalk Q.point) y ≠ 0 := by
314	      intro Q y hy; simp [hy]
315	    have key_alpha_ge : ∀ (Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor),
316	        0 ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q
317	              (algebraMap kbar C.left.functionField c) := by
318	      intro Q
319	      set R := C.left.presheaf.stalk Q.point
320	      let β : R := (C.left.presheaf.germ (⊤ : C.left.Opens) Q.point trivial).hom
321	        ((Scheme.toModuleKSheaf.kToSection C
322	            (Opposite.op (⊤ : C.left.Opens))).hom c)
323	      have hα_eq : (algebraMap kbar C.left.functionField c) =
324	          algebraMap R C.left.functionField β := by
325	        change ((Scheme.germToFunctionField C.left (⊤ : C.left.Opens)).hom

419	/-- **Type-level Subfunctor presentation of the carrier** (iter-189 Subfunctor
420	restructure). The carrier of `𝒪_C(P)` viewed as a `CategoryTheory.Subfunctor` of
421	the Type-valued presheaf `TopCat.presheafToType C.left.toTopCat C.left.functionField`
422	(the presheaf of arbitrary functions to the function field `K(C)`). A section over
423	an open `U` is a function `g : U.unop → K(C)` that is constant with value
424	`f ∈ carrierSubmoduleSheaf U`.

426	This Subfunctor packages the carrier conditions uniformly:
427	- At `U ≠ ⊥`: a section is a constant function valued in
428	  `carrierSubmoduleSheaf U = carrierSubmodule U` (so the order conditions hold).
429	- At `U = ⊥`: the unique empty function (witnessed by `f = 0 ∈ ⊥`).

432	sheaf `TopCat.Presheaf.toType_isSheaf` for `presheafToType`, reduces the sheaf
433	condition for the (type-valued shadow of the) carrier to a stalk-locality check:
434	every section of `presheafToType K(C)` whose sieve-of-section is covering already
435	lies in the Subfunctor. On the irreducible scheme `C.left`, this stalk-locality
436	holds because any two non-empty opens intersect, forcing constant-function gluing
437	to agree on overlaps, and the per-prime-divisor order conditions transfer from
438	each open in the cover to their union pointwise. -/

440	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
441	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsIntegral C.left]
442	    [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
443	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
444	    (P : C.left) (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1) :
445	    CategoryTheory.Subfunctor
446	      (TopCat.presheafToType C.left.toTopCat C.left.functionField) where
447	  obj U := { g : U.unop → C.left.functionField |
448	    ∃ f : C.left.functionField,
449	      f ∈ lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSubmoduleSheaf P hPcoh U ∧
450	        g = fun _ => f }
451	  map := by
452	    classical
453	    intro U V i g hg
454	    obtain ⟨f, hf, hgf⟩ := hg
455	    by_cases hV : V.unop = (⊥ : TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)
456	    · -- `V = ⊥`: `V.unop` is the empty type. Any function with empty domain works;

458	      refine ⟨0, ?_, ?_⟩
459	      · exact (lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSubmoduleSheaf P hPcoh V).zero_mem
460	      · funext x

889	
890	Hartshorne II §7 Proposition 7.7 identifies the global sections of
891	`ℒ(D_0)` with the rational functions `f ∈ K(X)^×` satisfying
892	`div(f) ≥ −D_0` (plus the zero section). Specialised to `D_0 = [P]` on a
893	curve, the condition `div(f) + [P] ≥ 0` rewrites coordinate-wise as
894	`ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor `Q ≠ P` and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1`. -/

896	/-- **Forward direction of `globalSections_iff` (Hartshorne II.7.7(b)).**

898	Given `f ∈ K(C)^×` with `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor `Q ≠ P`

900	`s ∈ H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P))` whose image under the canonical inclusion
901	`𝒪_C(P) ↪ 𝒦_C ≅ K(C)` equals `f`. Hartshorne's construction inside the
902	proof of Proposition 7.7(b) (p. 157) defines this section locally on the
903	affine cover witnessing the Cartier-divisor structure of `[P]`: on a
904	neighbourhood `U_i` of `P` the section is `f · f_P` (in
905	`𝒪_C(P)(U_i) = f_P⁻¹ · 𝒪_C(U_i)`, this is `(f · f_P) · f_P⁻¹ = f`);
906	on the complement `C ∖ {P}` the section is `f` directly (the order
907	conditions `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for `Q ≠ P` guarantee `f ∈ 𝒪_C(U) = 𝒪_C(P)(U)`

1001

1003	  the structure sheaf (since `𝒪_C(P)` equals `𝒪_C` on the open

1007	  uniformiser of the DVR `𝒪_{C, P}`); the germ of `s` at `P` lies in
1008	  this stalk, so `f = ι(s)` satisfies `f_P · f ∈ 𝒪_{C, P}`, i.e.

1356	/-- **Stacks 02P0 / Hartshorne I.3.4 substrate helper.**
1357	
1358	A nonzero rational function `f : K(C)` on a smooth proper geometrically
1359	irreducible curve `C / k̄` (with `k̄` algebraically closed and `C`
1360	integral) whose order vanishes at every prime divisor is the image of a
1361	constant under `algebraMap kbar K(C)`:
1362	```
1363	(∀ Q : C.PrimeDivisor, ord_Q(f) = 0)  →  ∃ c : kbar, f = algebraMap kbar K(C) c.
1364	```
1365	
1366	This is the classical Hartshorne I.3.4 statement (`Γ(C, 𝒪_C) = k̄` on a
1367	complete geom-irreducible variety over an algebraically closed field)
1368	combined with the "no zeros / no poles ⟹ globally regular" form of
1369	Stacks 02P0. **Mathlib gap** as of snapshot `b80f227`: Mathlib does not
1370	ship `Scheme.functionField_const_of_proper_geometricallyIrreducible` or
1371	equivalent. Closure requires two ingredients:
1372	
1373	1. **Algebraic Hartogs on a normal Noetherian scheme**: a rational function
1374	   regular at every codim-1 point extends to a global section of the
1375	   structure sheaf (Stacks tag `0BCK`). On a smooth curve `C`, every prime
1376	   divisor is a codim-1 point.
1377	2. **Γ(C, 𝒪_C) = k̄ for proper geom-irreducible curves over alg-closed k̄**
1378	   (Hartshorne I.3.4): a proper geom-irreducible reduced variety over an
1379	   alg-closed field has its global sections of the structure sheaf coinciding
1380	   with the base field, via the finite-dimensionality of Γ over `k̄`
1381	   (cohomological argument, Hartshorne III.5.2) plus algebraic closure
1382	   forcing all elements to be roots of polynomials, hence in `k̄`.
1383	
1384	Both ingredients are project-bespoke Mathlib gaps. The current iter leaves
1385	this as a single typed sorry; the named-helper extraction makes the
1386	upstream dependency explicit for future iterations.
1387	
1388	iter-196 Lane A: extraction of the inline `hf_const` sorry from
1389	`exists_nonconstant_rational_from_dim_eq_two`. -/
1390	private lemma functionField_const_of_complete_curve_of_orderZero
1391	    [∀ Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor,
1392	        Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (C.left.presheaf.stalk Q.point)]
1393	    (f : C.left.functionField) (_hf : f ≠ 0)
1394	    (_hord : ∀ Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor,
1395	        Scheme.RationalMap.order Q f = 0) :
1396	    ∃ c : kbar, f = algebraMap kbar C.left.functionField c := by
1397	  -- Stacks 02P0 / Hartshorne I.3.4: two-step closure as documented above.
1398	  --
1399	  -- Step (i): "algebraic Hartogs" extension to a global section. The
1400	  -- hypothesis `_hord : ∀ Q, ord_Q(f) = 0` says `f` has no zeros and no
1401	  -- poles at any prime divisor. On a smooth curve `C / k̄`, every codim-1
1402	  -- point is a prime divisor (the `IsRegularInCodimensionOne` instance
1403	  -- ensures the DVR stalks at those points and identifies `ord_Q` with
1404	  -- the standard valuation). Algebraic Hartogs (Stacks 0BCK; on a
1405	  -- normal Noetherian scheme, `Γ(C, 𝒪_C) = ⋂_{Q codim 1} 𝒪_{C, Q}`)
1406	  -- then exhibits `f` as a global section of `𝒪_C`.
1407	  --
1408	  -- Step (ii): `Γ(C, 𝒪_C) = k̄`. On a proper geom-irreducible reduced
1409	  -- variety over an algebraically closed field, `Γ(C, 𝒪_C) → k̄` (the
1410	  -- structural inclusion of constants) is an isomorphism. Hartshorne
1411	  -- I.3.4: a finite-dim `k̄`-algebra that is connected and reduced is a
1412	  -- product of fields; alg-closedness forces each factor to equal `k̄`;
1413	  -- connectedness forces a single factor; reducedness is automatic for

1638	with at most a simple pole at `P`** (Hartshorne IV §1 Exercise 1.1,

1641	Concretely, there exists `f ∈ K(C)` such that:
1642	
1643	* `f ≠ 0`;
1644	* `f ∉ k̄` (i.e. `f` is non-constant — for instance, it does not lie
1645	  in the image of the structural inclusion of constants);
1646	* `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor `Q ∈ C.PrimeDivisor` whose
1647	  generic point is not `P`;
1648	* `ord_P(f) ≥ −1` (at most a simple pole at `P`).
1649	
1650	iter-177+ body: use `dim_eq_two_of_genusZero` to get
1651	`dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P)) = 2`. The image of `1 ∈ H⁰(C, 𝒪_C) ≅ k̄`
1652	under the structural inclusion `𝒪_C ↪ 𝒪_C(P)` spans a one-dimensional
1653	subspace of `H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P))`; choose any section `s ∈ H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P))`
1654	not in this constant subspace (non-empty because `dim H⁰ = 2 > 1`),
1655	then take `f := lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField P hP s` and
1656	verify the four bullets via `globalSections_iff` applied to `f`
1657	(the forward direction supplies the order conditions from the existence
1658	witness `⟨s, rfl⟩`). The chosen `f` is non-constant because `s` is not
1659	in the constant subspace and `toFunctionField` is `k̄`-linear and
1660	injective on global sections.
1661

1663	f hf ≠ 0` follows from non-constancy plus the fact that constant
1664	functions have principal divisor zero (the converse — `div(f) = 0`
1665	⇒ `f` constant — uses the integrality of `C` and is the Stacks 02P0
1666	"functions with no zeros and poles are constant" type statement).

3	\chapter{The line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) and its global sections (RR.3)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.6, p.~144 (definition
7	% of \(\mathscr L(D)\)); II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective divisors
8	% as divisors of zeros of global sections); IV.1, pp.~294--297
9	% (Riemann--Roch, Lemma~1.2, Examples~1.3.3--1.3.5)
10	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161,
11	% 174, 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks
12	% below were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images

14	
15	\section{Setup and motivation}
16	
17	This chapter is the third of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
18	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
19	\[
20	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
21	  \,\colon\,
22	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
23	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
24	\]
25	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
26	the Weil-divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\), the degree map $\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C)
27	\to \mathbb Z\(, and the divisor \)[P]$ of a closed point
28	(\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the Riemann--Roch
29	dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
30	\[
31	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
32	\]
33	via the Euler-characteristic identity
34	(\texttt{RR.2}, REF); the global-sections
35	calculation \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a smooth
36	proper genus-\(0\) curve (this chapter, \texttt{RR.3}); the
37	``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)'' classification via
38	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
39	
40	The contribution of \texttt{RR.3} is twofold. First, it constructs the
41	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) associated to a closed point \(P \in C\)
42	on a smooth proper curve, identifies its \(\bar k\)-vector space of global
43	sections with the \emph{Riemann--Roch space}
44	\[
45	  L([P]) \;=\;
46	  \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0\bigr\}
47	  \,\cup\, \{0\},
48	\]
49	and computes its dimension as \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\)
50	in the genus-\(0\) specialisation. Second, it deduces from the
51	two-dimensionality that \(C\) admits a non-constant rational function \(f\)
52	having at most a simple pole at \(P\) and no other poles. This rational
53	function is the seed for \texttt{RR.4}: the pair $(1, f) \in
54	H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))^{\oplus 2}$ generates the sheaf and, via
55	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}, produces the morphism
56	\(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) whose degree-\(1\) identification is the
57	content of \texttt{RR.4}.
58	
59	% NOTE (Direct cohomological vanishing vs. Serre duality — iter-174
60	% sub-phase choice). The dimension computation
61	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) would, in Hartshorne's
62	% original presentation, route through Serre duality
63	% \(H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*\)
64	% to vanish \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) from the degree count
65	% \(\deg(K_C - [P]) = 2g - 2 - 1 = -3 < 0\). Mathlib (snapshot
66	% \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme and
67	% no Serre duality theorem. The genus-\(0\) specialisation does \emph{not}
68	% require Serre duality: the vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\) is
69	% delivered by the long exact sequence of the structural short exact
70	% sequence
71	% \[
72	%   0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
73	% \]
74	% together with the genus-\(0\) hypothesis
75	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g = 0\) and the skyscraper
76	% identity \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\). The chapter therefore uses the direct
77	% cohomological vanishing route exclusively; Serre duality is left for
78	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter that handles the general-\(g\)
79	% statement.
80	
81	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
82	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
83	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
84	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean}) carry the same
85	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
86	\begin{quote}
87	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
88	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
89	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
90	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
91	\end{quote}
92	The genus \(g(C)\) is the one defined in REF of
93	\texttt{Genus.tex}; the divisor \([P]\) of a closed point is the one of
94	REF of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex};
95	the principal divisor \(\div(f)\) of a nonzero rational function and the
96	order \(\ord_Q(f)\) at a prime divisor are the ones of
97	REF and REF.

123	    \longrightarrow \ModuleCat_{\bar k},
124	    \qquad U \longmapsto \mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(U),
125	  \]
126	  with restriction \(V \subseteq U \mapsto \texttt{Submodule.inclusion}\,
127	  (\texttt{carrierSet\_mono})\) on each pair. The functor laws follow from

154	\begin{definition}
155	
156	\leanok
157	[Carrier-submodule refinement enforcing \(F(\bot)=0\)]
158	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSubmoduleSheaf}
160	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf}
161	  Iter-188 Lane A discovery: the raw \(\mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P\) of
162	  \cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf} satisfies
163	  \(\mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(\bot) = K(C)\) (since the order conditions
164	  are vacuously satisfied on the empty open). This violates the sheaf
165	  axiom on the Grothendieck topology of opens: the empty sieve covers
166	  \(\bot\), forcing \(F(\bot) =\) terminal \(= 0\) on
167	  \(\ModuleCat_{\bar k}\)-valued functors.
168	  The refinement
169	  \[
170	    \mathrm{carrierSubmoduleSheaf}_P(U) \;:=\;
171	    \mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(U) \;\sqcap\; \mathrm{trivAtBot}\,U,
172	    \qquad \mathrm{trivAtBot}\,U \;:=\;
173	    \{\, f \;\mid\; U \ne \bot \,\lor\, f = 0 \,\}.
174	  \]
175	  forces \(F(\bot) = \bot\) (intersection annihilates) while preserving
176	  \(F(U)\) on non-empty \(U\) (since then \(\mathrm{trivAtBot}\,U = \top\)).
177	  The case-based restriction map (zero target on \(\bot\); inclusion
178	  otherwise) gives a well-formed sheaf-condition substrate consumed by
179	  \cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf_isSheaf}. Downstream
180	  consumers \texttt{lineBundleAtClosedPoint}, \texttt{toFunctionField},
181	  \texttt{globalSections\_iff\_*} are updated to supply the
182	  \texttt{trivAtBot} witness at \(\op\,\top\) via the \texttt{htop\_ne\_bot}
183	  helper.

186	\begin{definition}
187	
188	
189	\leanok
190	[Line bundle of a closed point]
191	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
192	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
193	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf, lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf_isSheaf}
194	  
195	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of the invertible
196	  % sheaf \(\mathscr L(D)\) associated to a Cartier divisor) (read from
197	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
198	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
199	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
200	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
201	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
202	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
203	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), and \(f_i^{-1}\) and
204	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
205	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
206	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.13. \emph{Let \(X\) be a scheme. Then:
207	  % (a) for any Cartier divisor \(D\), \(\mathscr L(D)\) is an invertible
208	  % sheaf on \(X\).}"
209	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of
210	  \(\mathscr L(D)\) and Proposition~6.13(a)).}
211	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a
212	  closed point. Let \(\mathfrak m_P \subset \mathcal O_{C, P}\) denote the
213	  maximal ideal of the local ring at \(P\); by smoothness of \(C\),
214	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}\) is a discrete valuation ring and \(\mathfrak m_P\)
215	  is generated by any uniformizer \(f_P \in \mathcal O_{C, P}\) (an
216	  element of \(\mathfrak m_P \setminus \mathfrak m_P^2\)). The Cartier
217	  divisor associated to the closed point \(P\) is the effective
218	  Cartier divisor cut out locally by the uniformizer \(f_P\) on an open
219	  affine neighbourhood of \(P\), and by the unit \(1\) on the complement
220	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). The \emph{line bundle of the closed point \(P\)},
221	  denoted \(\mathcal O_C(P)\), is the locally free \(\mathcal O_C\)-module
222	  of rank \(1\) defined by Hartshorne's construction \(\mathscr L(D)\)
223	  applied to this Cartier divisor: explicitly, \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the
224	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the sheaf of total quotient rings
225	  \(\mathcal K_C\) (the constant sheaf of the function field \(K(C)\) on the
226	  integral scheme \(C\)) that is generated by \(f_P^{-1} \in K(C)\) on a
227	  neighbourhood of \(P\) and by \(1 \in K(C)\) on the complement
228	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) confirms that
229	  this subsheaf is an invertible sheaf, independently of the choice of
230	  uniformizer \(f_P\) (any two uniformizers differ by a unit in
231	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}^{\times}\), so they generate the same
232	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-submodule of \(\mathcal K_C\)).
233	
234	  \paragraph{Equivalent description via the ideal sheaf.} Let
235	  \(\mathcal I_P \subset \mathcal O_C\) denote the ideal sheaf of the
236	  closed point \(P\) (locally generated by the uniformizer \(f_P\) near
237	  \(P\), and equal to \(\mathcal O_C\) on the complement). Then
238	  \(\mathcal I_P\) is the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(-P)\) associated to the
239	  Weil divisor \(-[P]\), and \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the
240	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-linear dual:
241	  \[
242	    \mathcal O_C(P) \;\cong\; \mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
243	    \mathcal O_C)
244	    \;\cong\; \mathcal I_P^{-1}.
245	  \]
246	  Both descriptions yield the same invertible sheaf; the
247	  ``subsheaf of \(\mathcal K_C\)'' description above is the one used in
248	  the prose, while the ``dual of the ideal sheaf'' description is the
249	  most direct entry point for the Lean construction (Mathlib's
250	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData} packages
251	  quasi-coherent ideal sheaves, from which the dual is constructed via
252	  the internal-hom on invertible sheaves).
253	
254	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
255	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint} takes a
256	  scheme \(C\), a point \(P : C\), and a closedness witness
257	  \(\texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C)}\) (the same input shape as
258	  REF), and returns an invertible
259	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-module \(\mathcal O_C(P)\). The well-definedness as an
260	  invertible sheaf is part of the construction and goes through
261	  smoothness of \(C\) (delivering the DVR stalk at \(P\)) and the standard
262	  ideal-sheaf-of-a-closed-point machinery in Mathlib's
263	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf} chapter. The chapter's
264	  consumption of the closed-point hypothesis matches \texttt{RR.1}:
265	  closed plus dimension-\(1\)-integral delivers \(\mathrm{codim}_C \{P\} = 1\)
266	  automatically. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
267	\end{definition}

269	\begin{definition}
270	
271	\leanok
272	[Inclusion of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) into the function field]
273	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}
274	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField}
275	  
276	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of \(\mathscr L(D)\) as a
277	  % subsheaf of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\); the
278	  % canonical inclusion \(\mathscr L(D) \hookrightarrow \mathscr K\) is part
279	  % of the construction) (read from
280	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
281	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
282	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
283	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
284	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
285	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
286	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), and \(f_i^{-1}\) and
287	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
288	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
289	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of \(\mathscr L(D)\)
290	  as a subsheaf of \(\mathscr K\)).}
291	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\)
292	  with \(C\) integral, and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. The line bundle
293	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) of REF is by
294	  construction a sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the sheaf of total quotient
295	  rings \(\mathcal K_C\). Since \(C\) is integral, \(\mathcal K_C\) is the
296	  constant sheaf at the function field \(K(C)\), and taking global sections
297	  of the structural inclusion \(\mathcal O_C(P) \hookrightarrow \mathcal K_C\)

299	  \[
300	    \iota \;\colon\; H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))

304	  function on \(C\) obtained by composing \(s\) with the inclusion of

306	  injective (the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module $\mathcal O_C(P) \subset
307	  \mathcal K_C\( is torsion-free of rank \)1$, so taking global sections

323	\section{Global sections as a Riemann--Roch space}
324	
325	\begin{lemma}
326	
327	
328	\leanok
329	[Global sections of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) as rational functions with a
330	controlled pole]
331	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.globalSections_iff}
333	  
334	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective
335	  % divisors linearly equivalent to \(D_0\) are the divisors of zeros of
336	  % global sections of \(\mathscr L(D_0)\), with the explicit
337	  % identification of those global sections with rational functions
338	  % \(f\) satisfying \((f) \geq -D_0\)) (read from
339	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 174).
340	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 7.7. \emph{Let \(X\) be a nonsingular
341	  % projective variety over the algebraically closed field \(k\). Let
342	  % \(D_0\) be a divisor on \(X\) and let \(\mathscr L = \mathscr L(D_0)\)
343	  % be the corresponding invertible sheaf. Then:
344	  % (a) for each nonzero \(s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\), the divisor of
345	  % zeros \((s)_0\) is an effective divisor linearly equivalent to \(D_0\);
346	  % (b) every effective divisor linearly equivalent to \(D_0\) is \((s)_0\)
347	  % for some \(s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\); and
348	  % (c) two sections \(s, s' \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\) have the same
349	  % divisor of zeros if and only if there is a \(\lambda \in k^*\) such
350	  % that \(s' = \lambda s\).}"
351	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(b) If \(D \geq 0\) and \(D = D_0 + (f)\), then
352	  % \((f) \geq -D_0\). Thus \(f\) gives a global section of
353	  % \(\mathscr L(D_0)\) whose divisor of zeros is \(D\)."
354	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.7, Proposition~7.7 and its
355	  proof of part (b), p.~157.}
356	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\), let \(P \in C\) be a
357	  closed point, and let \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) be the line bundle of
358	  REF. The \(\bar k\)-vector space of
359	  global sections of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is naturally identified with the
360	  Riemann--Roch space
361	  \[
362	    L([P]) \;:=\;
363	    \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0
364	    \text{ in } \mathrm{Div}(C)\bigr\}
365	    \,\cup\, \{0\}.
366	  \]
367	  Equivalently, the order conditions
368	  \[
369	    \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; 0 \quad \text{for every closed point } Q \in C
370	    \text{ with } Q \neq P,
371	    \qquad
372	    \ord_P(f) \;\geq\; -1
373	  \]
374	  on \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\) are equivalent to the existence of a global
375	  section \(s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) whose image \(\iota(s)\) under
376	  the canonical inclusion $\iota : \mathcal O_C(P) \hookrightarrow
377	  \mathcal K_C \cong K(C)\( equals \)f\( (the iff binds \)s\( to \)f$
378	  explicitly — vacuous-in-\(f\) readings such as ``some nonzero global
379	  section exists'' are excluded by construction).
380	
381	  Under this identification, the inclusion
382	  \(\bar k \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) of constant functions
383	  corresponds to the natural map $\mathcal O_C \hookrightarrow
384	  \mathcal O_C(P)\( exhibiting \)\mathcal O_C(P)$ as the twist of the
385	  structure sheaf along the prime divisor \([P]\).
386	\end{lemma}
387	
388	\begin{proof}
389	  
390	  
391	  
392	  
393	\leanok
394	  By construction, \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of
395	  the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathcal K_C\) generated locally by
396	  \(f_P^{-1}\) near \(P\) and by \(1\) on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), where \(f_P\) is
397	  a uniformizer at \(P\) (REF). Since \(C\)
398	  is integral, \(\mathcal K_C\) is the constant sheaf at the function field
399	  \(K(C)\), and a global section of any sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module
400	  \(\mathcal F \subset \mathcal K_C\) is precisely an element of \(K(C)\)
401	  whose germ at every point lies in \(\mathcal F\) at that point.
402	  Therefore \(f \in K(C)\) is a global section of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) if
403	  and only if:
404	
405	  \begin{itemize}
406	    \item For every closed point \(Q \in C\) with \(Q \neq P\), the germ of
407	      \(f\) at \(Q\) lies in \(\mathcal O_C(P)_Q = \mathcal O_{C, Q}\)
408	      (the structure sheaf, since \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) agrees with
409	      \(\mathcal O_C\) on \(C \setminus \{P\}\)); equivalently,
410	      \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\).
411	    \item At the closed point \(P\), the germ of \(f\) at \(P\) lies in
412	      \(\mathcal O_C(P)_P = f_P^{-1} \cdot \mathcal O_{C, P}\);
413	      equivalently, \(f_P \cdot f \in \mathcal O_{C, P}\), i.e.\
414	      \(\ord_P(f_P \cdot f) \geq 0\), i.e.\ \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\).
415	  \end{itemize}
416	  In divisorial language (REF,
417	  REF), the two conditions combine to
418	  \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\), recovering the Riemann--Roch space description
419	  \(L([P])\). The zero element of \(K(C)\) is included by convention as the
420	  zero global section.
421	
422	  The identification with Hartshorne's Proposition~7.7(b) is direct:
423	  taking \(D_0 = [P]\) effective, Hartshorne's argument shows that a
424	  rational function \(f\) with \((f) \geq -D_0\) defines a global section
425	  of \(\mathscr L(D_0)\). The condition \((f) \geq -D_0 = -[P]\) is exactly
426	  \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\). The compatibility with the constant inclusion
427	  follows by taking \(f = 1 \in K(C)^{\times}\): then \(\div(1) = 0\), so
428	  \(\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0\), and the corresponding section of
429	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the image of $1 \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
430	  \cong \bar k\( under the structural inclusion \)\mathcal O_C
431	  \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(P)$.
432	\end{proof}
