434	\section{Cohomological vanishing in genus zero}
435	
436	\begin{lemma}
437	
438	
439	\leanok
440	[Vanishing of \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
441	  \label{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
442	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.h1_vanishing_genusZero}
443	  
444	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the proof of Theorem~1.3 (the inductive
445	  % step ``Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any point.''):
446	  % the short exact sequence $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to
447	  % k(P) \to 0\( and its tensor with \)\mathscr L(D + P)$ giving
448	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\)
449	  % (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
450	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its
451	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\),
452	  % which we denote by \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\)
453	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
454	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
455	  % Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
456	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\),
457	  % (since \(\mathscr L(P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
458	  % does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\))."
459	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
460	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
461	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
462	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
463	  \[
464	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 0,
465	  \]
466	  i.e.\ \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\).
467	\end{lemma}
468	
469	\begin{proof}
470	  
471	  Specialise Hartshorne's inductive step at \(D = 0\). The standard short
472	  exact sequence
473	  \[
474	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
475	  \]
476	  (Hartshorne~II.6.18: the ideal sheaf of the locally principal closed
477	  subscheme \(P\) is \(\mathcal O_C(-[P])\); the quotient is the structure
478	  sheaf of \(P\), which is the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P) \cong \bar k\)
479	  supported at \(P\) since \(P\) has residue field \(\bar k\) by closedness
480	  over the algebraically closed base \(\bar k\)). Tensoring with the
481	  locally free rank-\(1\) sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\)
482	  (REF) preserves exactness and leaves
483	  the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) unchanged, giving
484	  \[
485	    0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
486	  \]
487	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\). Apply the long exact sequence of sheaf
488	  cohomology (REF, the project's $\Module \bar
489	  k\(-flavoured cohomology pipeline). Using that \)\dim C = 1$ kills
490	  \(H^i\) for \(i \geq 2\) (Grothendieck vanishing on a \(1\)-dimensional
491	  scheme), the long exact sequence reads
492	  \begin{multline*}
493	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^0(C, k(P)) \\
494	    \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^1(C, k(P)) \to 0.
495	  \end{multline*}
496	  Two ingredients close the proof:
497	
498	  \emph{(a) \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\).} This is the genus-\(0\)
499	  hypothesis: by REF,
500	  \[
501	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C),
502	  \]
503	  so \(g(C) = 0\) is exactly \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\),
504	  i.e.\ \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
505	
506	  \emph{(b) \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).} The skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) is
507	  supported at a single closed point, hence is flasque: every section
508	  on an open \(U \subset C\) either restricts to \(0\) (if \(P \notin U\)) or
509	  takes values in the stalk \(k(P)_P \cong \bar k\) (if \(P \in U\)), and
510	  restriction maps between opens of either type are surjective. A
511	  flasque sheaf on any topological space has vanishing higher
512	  cohomology (Hartshorne~III.2.5); in particular
513	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\), and \emph{a fortiori}
514	  \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
515	
516	  Substituting (a) and (b) into the long exact sequence collapses the
517	  segment ending at \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) to
518	  \[
519	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to 0,
520	  \]
521	  whence \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\), as required.
522	
523	  \emph{Lean reference note on the long exact sequence.} Mathlib
524	  (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the long exact sequence of
525	  \(\Ext_{\mathcal O_C}^*(\mathbf 1, -)\) at the abelian-group level
526	  (\texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact}); the
527	  project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured cohomology pipeline
528	  (REF) inherits the long exact sequence by
529	  forget-functor naturality. The skyscraper-cohomology vanishing
530	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\) is closed by either the flasque
531	  argument (Hartshorne~III.2.5; Mathlib has the abelian-sheaf form
532	  via \texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheaf.Flasque}) or, more directly for the
533	  curve case, by the affine-cover Čech-cohomology argument on
534	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and a small affine open around \(P\) (the
535	  skyscraper has zero sections on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), so the Čech
536	  complex degenerates).
537	\end{proof}

539	\section{The dimension formula in genus zero}
540	
541	% NOTE (iter-194 reviewer): The Lean body is now structurally
542	% sorry-free (closed transitively through two named substrate
543	% helpers). The substrate helpers `h0_sub_h1_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_eq_two`
544	% (the Int-valued $H^0 - H^1 = 2$ \(\chi\)-bridge; gated on
545	% `OcOfD.lean` STRUCTURALLY BLOCKED + `RRFormula.lean` $\chi$-additivity)
546	% and `h1_vanishing_genusZero` (gated on Lane H \texttt{H1Vanishing.lean}
547	% substrate) carry the remaining typed sorries.  Future consumers
548	% reading the body should consult those.
549	\begin{theorem}
550	
551	
552	\leanok
553	[\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
554	  \label{thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
555	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
556	  
557	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the Riemann--Roch chain inside
558	  % Example~1.3.5 applied with \(D = [P]\): $\chi(\mathscr L([P]))
559	  % = \deg([P]) + 1 - g = 1 + 1 - 0 = 2$, combined with the vanishing
560	  % \(H^1(\mathscr L([P])) = 0\) (read from
561	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 312, 314).
562	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let \(D\) be a
563	  % divisor on a curve \(X\) of genus \(g\). Then}
564	  % \(l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g\)."
565	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
566	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
567	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
568	  % is the Euler characteristic
569	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
570	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295 (the
571	  \(\chi\)-identity inside the proof) and Example~1.3.5, p.~297 (the
572	  genus-\(0\) specialisation chain).}
573	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
574	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
575	  \[
576	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 2.
577	  \]
578	\end{theorem}
579	
580	\begin{proof}
581	  
582	  
583	  
584	\leanok
585	  Specialise the Euler-characteristic identity
586	  REF of \texttt{RR.2} to
587	  the divisor \(D = [P]\) of REF. The
588	  degree of \([P]\) is \(\deg([P]) = 1\) (every closed point contributes
589	  \(1\) to the bare-sum degree of REF), and the
590	  genus hypothesis \(g(C) = 0\) (REF) gives
591	  \[
592	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; \deg([P]) + 1 - g(C)
593	    \;=\; 1 + 1 - 0 \;=\; 2.
594	  \]
595	  Unfolding \(\chi\) via REF as a two-term Euler
596	  characteristic on a curve, this rewrites as
597	  \[
598	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
599	    \,-\, \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
600	    \;=\; 2.
601	  \]
602	  By
603	  REF, the second
604	  term vanishes:
605	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\). Substituting yields
606	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\), as required.
607	
608	  \emph{Alternative direct route via the long exact sequence.} The
609	  same long exact sequence used in the proof of
610	  REF computes
611	  the dimension of \(H^0\) in one stroke without invoking
612	  \texttt{RR.2}: from
613	  \[
614	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
615	    \to H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to \cdots
616	  \]
617	  together with \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1\)
618	  (Hartshorne~I.3.4: \(H^0\) of the structure sheaf on a proper
619	  geometrically irreducible variety over \(\bar k\) is \(\bar k\)),
620	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) (skyscraper at a \(\bar k\)-rational
621	  point), and \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) (genus-\(0\)), the connecting
622	  map \(H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) has zero codomain and the
623	  segment yields the short exact sequence
624	  \[
625	    0 \to \bar k \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to \bar k \to 0
626	  \]
627	  of \(\bar k\)-vector spaces, whence $\dim_{\bar k}
628	  H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 1 + 1 = 2$. This alternative route does
629	  not consume the Euler-characteristic identity of \texttt{RR.2} and
630	  could be substituted at the formalisation stage; the chapter uses
631	  \texttt{RR.2} as written above to keep the dependency graph in
632	  line with the iter-173 RR sub-build sequencing.
633	\end{proof}

824	\begin{lemma}
825	[Sub-claim (c) substrate: a rational function with zero order at every

855	% geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\)'') — and inlined the
856	% contrapositive ``\(s \notin \bar k\cdot s_1 \Rightarrow
857	% \mathrm{principal}\,f \ne 0\)'' wrapper inside

859	% (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean} lines~1596--1635).

861	% = 0\); apply the substrate to obtain \(f = c \in \bar k\); use
862	% \(\bar k\)-linearity of \(\iota\) plus \(\iota(s_1) = 1\) and

863	% injectivity of \(\iota\)
864	% (\cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective}) to
865	% conclude \(s = c\cdot s_1 \in \bar k\cdot s_1\), contradicting
866	% \(s \notin \bar k\cdot s_1\).

868	\begin{proof}
869	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
870	  This is the classical Hartshorne~I.3.4 statement
871	  ``\(\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) = \bar k\) on a complete geometrically
872	  irreducible reduced variety over the algebraically closed field
873	  \(\bar k\)'', combined with the
874	  ``no-zeros-no-poles \(\Rightarrow\) regular'' content of
875	  Stacks~\texttt{02P0}, in two ingredients:
876	
877	  \emph{Step (i) — algebraic Hartogs.} On the normal Noetherian scheme
878	  \(C\), a rational function regular at every codimension-\(1\) point
879	  extends uniquely to a global section of the structure sheaf
880	  (Stacks tag~\texttt{0BCK}: $\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) =
881	  \bigcap_{Q\in C^{(1)}} \mathcal O_{C, Q}$). On the smooth curve
882	  \(C\) every prime divisor \(Q\) is a codimension-\(1\) point and
883	  \(\ord_Q\) is the standard discrete valuation on
884	  \(\mathcal O_{C, Q}\); the hypothesis \(\ord_Q(f) = 0\) for every
885	  \(Q\) places \(f\) in the intersection
886	  \(\bigcap_Q \mathcal O_{C, Q} = \Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C)\).
887	
888	  \emph{Step (ii) — global sections are constants.} On a proper
889	  geometrically irreducible reduced variety over the algebraically
890	  closed field \(\bar k\), the global-sections ring of the structure
891	  sheaf coincides with the base field,
892	  \(\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) = \bar k\) (Hartshorne~I.3.4; or
893	  cohomologically: \(\Gamma\) is finite-dimensional over \(\bar k\)
894	  by Hartshorne~III.5.2, hence integral over \(\bar k\), hence
895	  algebraic, hence \(\bar k\) by algebraic closure). Combining
896	  step~(i) with step~(ii) places \(f\) in the image of the constant
897	  inclusion \(\bar k \hookrightarrow K(C)\), as required.
898	
899	  \emph{Mathlib status.} At snapshot \texttt{b80f227} both ingredients
900	  are project-bespoke Mathlib gaps: the Lean substrate helper carries
901	  a single typed sorry pending upstream development; the named-helper
902	  extraction makes the upstream dependency explicit for future
903	  iterations.
904	\end{proof}
905	
906	\section{Existence of a non-constant rational function}
907	
908	\begin{corollary}
909	
910	
911	\leanok
912	[A genus-\(0\) curve admits a non-constant rational function with a
913	single simple pole]
914	  \label{cor:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
915	  % Alias label consumed under the shorter name by
916	  % \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex} (RR.4):
917	  \label{cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero}
918	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
919	  
920	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Exercise~1.1 (the existence of a
921	  % nonconstant rational function regular everywhere except at one
922	  % point on a genus-\(0\) curve, as a direct consequence of the
923	  % Riemann--Roch dimension formula) (read from
924	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
925	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "1.1. Let \(X\) be a curve, and let \(P \in X\) be a
926	  % point. Then there exists a nonconstant rational function $f \in
927	  % K(X)\(, which is regular everywhere except at \)P$."
928	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Exercise~1.1, p.~297.}
929	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
930	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
931	  there exists a non-constant rational function \(f \in K(C)\) such that:
932	  \begin{itemize}
933	    \item \(f\) is regular on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), i.e.\ \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\)
934	      for every closed point \(Q \in C\) with \(Q \neq P\);
935	    \item \(f\) has at most a simple pole at \(P\), i.e.\
936	      \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\);
937	    \item \(f \notin \bar k\), equivalently
938	      \(\div(f) \neq 0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\).
939	  \end{itemize}
940	  In particular, the pair \((1, f)\) spans
941	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
942	\end{corollary}
943	
944	\begin{proof}
945	  
946	  
947	  
948	\leanok
949	  By
950	  REF,
951	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\). The constant subspace
952	  \(\bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\), obtained from the
953	  structural inclusion $\bar k \cong H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
954	  \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ (the rational function
955	  \(f = 1\), with \(\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0\), under the
956	  identification of
957	  REF), is a one-dimensional
958	  \(\bar k\)-vector subspace. Since the ambient space has dimension \(2\),
959	  the quotient \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) / \bar k \cdot 1\) is a
960	  non-zero one-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector space; any lift to
961	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) of a non-zero element of the quotient is
962	  an element of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k \cdot 1\).
963	
964	  Choose such a lift $s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k
965	  \cdot 1$. Under the identification of
966	  REF the section
967	  \(s\) corresponds to a rational function \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\)
968	  satisfying \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\), equivalently \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\)
969	  for every \(Q \neq P\) and \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\). The choice
970	  \(s \notin \bar k \cdot 1\) means \(f\) is not the image of a constant,
971	  so \(f \notin \bar k\) (every non-zero element of
972	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \cong \bar k\) corresponds, under the
973	  identification, to a constant element of \(K(C)\)). The three bullets
974	  of the statement are now immediate.
975	
976	  Spanning. Linear independence of \(1\) and \(f\) over \(\bar k\) in
977	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) is the same condition as
978	  \(s \notin \bar k \cdot 1\) above; combined with the dimension count
979	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\), the pair \((1, f)\) is a
980	  \(\bar k\)-basis of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\).
981	
982	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean signature
983	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists\_nonconstant\_genusZero}
984	  returns an element \(f\) of the project's function-field carrier
985	  \(K(C)\) together with proofs of regularity on \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and
986	  the simple-pole bound at \(P\), packaged in the same shape as
987	  REF's output. The
988	  non-constancy is recorded as a separate field; the spanning
989	  statement is downstream and is consumed by \texttt{RR.4} via
990	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}.
991	\end{proof}
992	
993	\section{Mathlib-status flag: the H\(^1\)-vanishing input}
994	
995	The vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\) on a genus-\(0\) curve
996	(REF) is the
997	critical cohomological input of this chapter. Its closure depends on
998	two Mathlib-status facts, both of which the project already consumes
999	elsewhere:
1000	
1001	\begin{itemize}
1002	  \item \textbf{Long exact sequence of \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured sheaf
1003	    cohomology.} Mathlib (\texttt{b80f227}) ships
1004	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact} for
1005	    \(\Ext^*\)-long-exact-sequences at the abelian-group level. The
1006	    project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured pipeline
1007	    (REF, \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex})
1008	    inherits it by forget-functor naturality. This bridge is already
1009	    consumed by REF of
1010	    \texttt{RR.2}, so closing it here introduces no new Mathlib
1011	    obligation.
1012	  \item \textbf{Vanishing of higher cohomology of a skyscraper sheaf}
1013	    on a Noetherian scheme. Hartshorne~III.2.5 gives the flasque
1014	    formulation; the project's affine-cover Čech route
1015	    (REF and surrounding
1016	    \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex} machinery) computes
1017	    the same vanishing for the skyscraper at a closed point by direct
1018	    Čech-degeneration. \texttt{RR.2}'s inductive step already invokes
1019	    \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), which is the combined statement
1020	    \(\dim H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) and \(\dim H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\),
1021	    so the vanishing \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\) is already a consumer
1022	    obligation of \texttt{RR.2}.
1023	\end{itemize}
1024	
1025	Consequently the H\(^1\)-vanishing
1026	REF introduces no
1027	Mathlib obligation that is not already on \texttt{RR.2}'s critical
1028	path. The genus-\(0\) specialisation should therefore close at the
1029	formalisation stage without splitting this chapter or pushing the
1030	vanishing back into \texttt{RR.2}.
1031	
1032	% NOTE (iter-174 plan-agent flag). The general-\(g\) vanishing statement
1033	% ``\(H^1(C, \mathcal L) = 0\) whenever \(\deg \mathcal L > 2g - 2\)''
1034	% (Hartshorne~IV.1, the Riemann--Roch consequence read covariantly) is
1035	% \emph{not} a deliverable of this chapter and is not required for the
1036	% genus-\(0\) critical path: the genus-\(0\) argument closes via the long
1037	% exact sequence at \(D = 0\) without ever needing the general bound. The
1038	% general bound is a Serre-duality-flavoured statement and is queued for
1039	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter together with the dualizing sheaf
1040	% \(\omega_C\) and the canonical-divisor identification \(K_C \sim -2[P]\)
1041	% on \(\mathbb P^1\) (Hartshorne~II.8.20.1).
1042	
1043	\section{Out of scope}
1044	
1045	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.3}
1046	and are sequenced into the sibling sub-build chapters or queued for
1047	follow-up iterations:
1048	
1049	\begin{itemize}
1050	  \item The construction of the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)
1051	    associated to an arbitrary Weil divisor $D = \sum n_i [P_i] \in
1052	    \mathrm{Div}(C)$, and the linear-equivalence isomorphism
1053	    \(\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')\) when \(D \sim D'\), are
1054	    \emph{not} performed in this chapter. The chapter constructs only
1055	    the specialisation \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) at \(D = [P]\) a prime divisor,
1056	    which is the only case consumed by the genus-\(0\) classification
1057	    chain. The general \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is queued for the
1058	    post-RR-bridge follow-up (where it pairs with the general-\(g\)
1059	    Riemann--Roch formula and the canonical-divisor identification).
1060	  \item The general-\(g\) statement
1061	    $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = \deg([P]) + 1 -
1062	    g(C) + \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$, while a direct
1063	    consequence of
1064	    REF of \texttt{RR.2},
1065	    is not separately formalised here; the chapter restricts to the
1066	    genus-\(0\) specialisation \(g = 0\) on which
1067	    REF is
1068	    available.
1069	  \item \textbf{Serre duality} $H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong
1070	    H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*$ and the associated
1071	    canonical divisor \(K_C = c_1(\omega_C)\) are \emph{not} used at any
1072	    step of this chapter and are not deliverables; the chapter routes
1073	    cohomological vanishing through the long exact sequence at
1074	    \(D = 0\) (REF)
1075	    instead. Serre duality is queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up.
1076	  \item The morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) produced by applying
1077	    \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} to the basis \((1, f)\) of
1078	    REF,
1079	    and its identification as an isomorphism, are the content of
1080	    \texttt{RR.4} (sibling chapter
1081	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalIsoP1.tex}, to be added). The present
1082	    chapter supplies the basis \((1, f)\) and stops.
1083	  \item The general-\(g\) vanishing
1084	    \(H^1(C, \mathcal L) = 0\) for \(\deg \mathcal L > 2g - 2\) is
1085	    \emph{not} part of this chapter and is not required for the
1086	    genus-\(0\) critical path. It is a Serre-duality-flavoured statement
1087	    queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up.
1088	  \item The interpretation of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) as the space
1089	    of sections of a complete linear system \(|[P]|\) in

100	/-- The **Weil divisor group** of a scheme `X` satisfying Hartshorne's condition
101	`(*)`: the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of `X`,
102	`Div(X) = ⨁_{Y prime divisor of X} ℤ · Y`. An element `D = Σ nᵢ · Yᵢ` with finitely
103	many nonzero coefficients is a **Weil divisor**; if all `nᵢ ≥ 0` it is **effective**.
104	
105	Blueprint reference: `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 130). -/
106	def Scheme.WeilDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type u := X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ
107	
108	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
109	
110	noncomputable instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : AddCommGroup X.WeilDivisor :=
111	  inferInstanceAs (AddCommGroup (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
112	
113	instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : Inhabited X.WeilDivisor :=
114	  inferInstanceAs (Inhabited (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
115	
116	end Scheme.WeilDivisor
117	
118	/-! ## §2. Order of a rational function at a prime divisor
119	
120	For a scheme `X` satisfying `(*)`, every prime divisor `Y` carries a discrete
121	valuation `v_Y` on the function field `K(X)`. The order of a nonzero rational
122	function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along `Y` is the integer `ord_Y(f) := v_Y(f)`. -/
123	
124	namespace Scheme.RationalMap
125	
126	/-- **Order of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along a prime divisor `Y`.**
127	
128	For `X` satisfying Hartshorne's `(*)`, the local ring `O_{X,η}` at the generic
129	point `η` of `Y` is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field the function
130	field `K(X)`, and `ord_Y(f) = v_Y(f) ∈ ℤ` is the value of the associated normalised
131	discrete valuation on `f`.
132	
133	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
134	divisor and `ord_P(f) = v_P(f)` is the standard DVR valuation at `P`.
135	
136	Blueprint reference: `def:order_at_point` (Hartshorne II §6 pp. 130–131; Stacks 02ME).
137	
138	iter-176 body (per analogist `dvr-rationalmap-order`): the body uses Mathlib's
139	`Ring.ordFrac` (the canonical `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` monoid-with-zero hom from
140	`Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic`, Stacks `02MD`) on the stalk
141	`X.presheaf.stalk Y.point`, then projects through `WithZero.log : ℤᵐ⁰ → ℤ`
142	(the canonical projection with junk-on-zero, `Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero`).
143	On `f = 0` this gives `order Y 0 = 0` (junk convention from `WithZero.log_zero`).
144	
145	The required Mathlib typeclasses on the stalk are:
146	- `IsNoetherianRing` — from `[IsLocallyNoetherian X]`.
147	- `IsDomain` ⟹ `Nontrivial` — from `[IsIntegral X]`.
148	- `IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) X.functionField` — from `[IsIntegral X]`.
149	- `Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` — threaded explicitly (the
150	  topological-coheight-to-Krull-dim bridge `Order.coheight Y.point = 1 ⟹
151	  Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` is a Mathlib-upstream-pending
152	  gap; see Stacks `02IZ` / `005X`). -/
153	noncomputable def order {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X]
154	    [IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
155	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]
156	    (f : X.functionField) : ℤ :=
157	  WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) f)
158	
159	end Scheme.RationalMap
160	
161	/-! ### Regular-in-codimension-one bridge class
162	
163	Hartshorne's condition `(*)` includes "regular in codimension one", i.e.\ every
164	prime divisor `Y` of `X` has a DVR stalk `O_{X,Y.point}`. Mathlib has no direct
165	typeclass for this; we package it as the project-bespoke class
166	`Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne`, with an instance synthesising the per-`Y`
167	Krull-dim-≤-1 condition required by `Scheme.RationalMap.order`. Blueprint pin
168	("Iter-173+ may introduce a `Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne` predicate to
169	abbreviate this"; chapter `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §2 "Standing hypothesis
170	`(*)` in the Lean encoding"). -/
171	
172	/-- Project-bespoke class encoding Hartshorne's "regular in codimension one"
173	clause of `(*)`: every prime divisor `Y` of `X` has a DVR stalk
174	`O_{X,Y.point}`. The `[IsIntegral X]` precondition makes
175	`IsDomain (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` available so that the
176	`IsDiscreteValuationRing` predicate is well-formed
177	(`AlgebraicGeometry.instIsDomainCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsIntegral`). -/
178	class Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne (X : Scheme.{u}) [IsIntegral X] : Prop where
179	  /-- The defining content: every prime divisor's stalk is a discrete valuation
180	  ring. (This is the precise content of Hartshorne's `(*)`; the weaker
181	  `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` derives via the `IsDiscreteValuationRing` ⟹
182	  `IsPrincipalIdealRing` ⟹ `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` chain via the bridge
183	  instance below.) -/
184	  out : ∀ Y : Scheme.PrimeDivisor X, IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)
185	
186	/-- Bridge instance: from `[Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]`, typeclass
187	synthesis can derive `IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` for
188	every prime divisor `Y`. -/
189	instance Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instIsDiscreteValuationRingStalk
190	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
191	    (Y : Scheme.PrimeDivisor X) :
192	    IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
193	  Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.out Y
194	
195	/-- Bridge instance: from `[Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]`, typeclass
196	synthesis can derive `Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` for every
197	prime divisor `Y`, via the `IsDiscreteValuationRing` ⟹ `IsPrincipalIdealRing`
198	⟹ `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` chain (Mathlib's `IsDiscreteValuationRing.toIsPrincipalIdealRing`
199	+ `IsPrincipalIdealRing.krullDimLE_one`). -/
200	instance Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instKrullDimLEStalk
201	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
202	    (Y : Scheme.PrimeDivisor X) :
203	    Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
204	  haveI : IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
205	    Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.out Y
206	  haveI : IsPrincipalIdealRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
207	    IsDiscreteValuationRing.toIsPrincipalIdealRing
208	  IsPrincipalIdealRing.krullDimLE_one _
209	
210	/-- **Hartshorne II.6.1**: for a nonzero rational function `f` on a Noetherian
211	integral scheme `X` satisfying `(*)`, the order function `Y ↦ ord_Y(f)` is
212	nonzero at only finitely many prime divisors `Y`. This is the well-definedness
213	side condition for `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal`.
214	
215	iter-177 status: this packages Hartshorne's Lemma 6.1, which Mathlib does not
216	ship. The body is a Mathlib-upstream-pending gap (Stacks tag `02RV` — for a
217	nonzero element `f ∈ K(X)^×` of a Noetherian integral scheme, only finitely
218	many height-one primes can divide either numerator or denominator); the proof
219	factors through `IsLocallyNoetherian X` + the principal-ideal generation of
220	height-1 primes + the finite irreducible-component decomposition of
221	`V(f₀) ∪ V(f∞)`. The chapter pins this as a separate sub-build deferral
222	(`RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §5).
223	
224	The statement is generic in `f` (no `f ≠ 0` hypothesis is threaded): on
225	`f = 0` the function `Y ↦ ord_Y(0) = WithZero.log 0 = 0` has empty support,
226	which is finite, so the conclusion holds vacuously. -/
227	private theorem rationalMap_order_finite_support {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X]
228	    [IsLocallyNoetherian X] [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
229	    (f : X.functionField) :
230	    (Function.support (fun Y : X.PrimeDivisor =>
231	      Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f)).Finite := by
232	  -- **iter-192 case split + f = 0 branch closed axiom-clean.**
233	  --
234	  -- Case 1 (f = 0): the order function evaluates to
235	  -- `WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac _ 0) = WithZero.log 0 = 0` at every
236	  -- prime divisor, so the support is empty (finite vacuously).
237	  by_cases hf : f = 0
238	  · subst hf
239	    convert Set.finite_empty
240	    ext Y
241	    simp only [Function.mem_support, ne_eq, Set.mem_empty_iff_false, iff_false,
242	      Decidable.not_not, Scheme.RationalMap.order, map_zero, WithZero.log_zero]
243	  -- Case 2 (f ≠ 0): genuinely needs Hartshorne II.6.1 (Stacks 02RV).
244	  -- The proof for nonzero `f` requires the substrate
245	  -- "for a Noetherian integral scheme, only finitely many height-1
246	  -- primes can divide the numerator or denominator of a nonzero rational
247	  -- function" — a Mathlib-pending gap noted in the chapter docstring.
248	  -- Body owed iter-193+; iter-192 closes only the f = 0 vacuous case.
249	  · sorry
250	
251	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
252	
253	variable {X : Scheme.{u}}
254	
255	/-! ## §3. Divisor of a closed point on a curve
256	
257	On a smooth proper curve `C` over a field, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
258	divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the one-dimensional integral
259	scheme `C`). The associated Weil divisor is `[P] = 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`. -/
260	
261	/-- **The Weil divisor associated to a closed point `P` on a curve.** The element
262	`[P] := 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`, i.e. the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one.
263	
264	For a smooth proper curve every closed point is automatically a prime divisor (it
265	is a codimension-one integral closed subscheme of the one-dimensional integral
266	scheme `C`); conversely every prime divisor on a curve is of this form, so an
267	arbitrary divisor on `C` is a finite formal `ℤ`-linear combination
268	`Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]` of closed points.

import Mathlib

open Ring in
example {R : Type*} [CommRing R] [IsDomain R] [IsDiscreteValuationRing R]
    {K : Type*} [Field K] [Algebra R K] [IsFractionRing R K]
    (x : K) (hx_ne : x ≠ 0)
    (hx_ord : WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac R x) = 0) :
    ∃ r : R, IsUnit r ∧ algebraMap R K r = x := by
  have hox_ne : Ring.ordFrac R x ≠ 0 := by
    intro h
    apply hx_ne
    exact (MonoidWithZeroHom.map_eq_zero (Ring.ordFrac R)).mp h
  have hox_eq_one : Ring.ordFrac R x = 1 := by
    have h1 : WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac R x) = WithZero.log (1 : WithZero (Multiplicative ℤ)) := by
      rw [hx_ord, WithZero.log_one]
    have hone_ne : (1 : WithZero (Multiplicative ℤ)) ≠ 0 := one_ne_zero
    have hle : Ring.ordFrac R x ≤ 1 := (WithZero.log_le_log hox_ne hone_ne).mp (by rw [h1])
    have hge : (1 : WithZero (Multiplicative ℤ)) ≤ Ring.ordFrac R x :=
      (WithZero.log_le_log hone_ne hox_ne).mp (by rw [← h1])
    exact le_antisymm hle hge
  -- Use `mker_ordFrac_eq_isUnitSubmonoid` to extract `r` from `ordFrac R x = 1`.
  have hmem : x ∈ MonoidHom.mker (Ring.ordFrac R) := by
    rw [MonoidHom.mem_mker]
    exact hox_eq_one
  rw [Ring.mker_ordFrac_eq_isUnitSubmonoid] at hmem
  obtain ⟨r, hr_mem, hr_map⟩ := hmem
  exact ⟨r, hr_mem, hr_map⟩

/-- **Stacks 02P0 / Hartshorne I.3.4 substrate helper.**

A nonzero rational function `f : K(C)` on a smooth proper geometrically
irreducible curve `C / k̄` (with `k̄` algebraically closed and `C`
integral) whose order vanishes at every prime divisor is the image of a
constant under `algebraMap kbar K(C)`:
```
(∀ Q : C.PrimeDivisor, ord_Q(f) = 0)  →  ∃ c : kbar, f = algebraMap kbar K(C) c.
```

This is the classical Hartshorne I.3.4 statement (`Γ(C, 𝒪_C) = k̄` on a
complete geom-irreducible variety over an algebraically closed field)
combined with the "no zeros / no poles ⟹ globally regular" form of
Stacks 02P0. **Mathlib gap** as of snapshot `b80f227`: Mathlib does not
ship `Scheme.functionField_const_of_proper_geometricallyIrreducible` or
equivalent. Closure requires two ingredients:

1. **Algebraic Hartogs on a normal Noetherian scheme**: a rational function
   regular at every codim-1 point extends to a global section of the
   structure sheaf (Stacks tag `0BCK`). On a smooth curve `C`, every prime
   divisor is a codim-1 point.
2. **Γ(C, 𝒪_C) = k̄ for proper geom-irreducible curves over alg-closed k̄**
   (Hartshorne I.3.4): a proper geom-irreducible reduced variety over an
   alg-closed field has its global sections of the structure sheaf coinciding
   with the base field, via the finite-dimensionality of Γ over `k̄`
   (cohomological argument, Hartshorne III.5.2) plus algebraic closure
   forcing all elements to be roots of polynomials, hence in `k̄`.

Both ingredients are project-bespoke Mathlib gaps. The current iter leaves
this as a single typed sorry; the named-helper extraction makes the
upstream dependency explicit for future iterations.

/-! ### Iter-197 substrate: from `order Q f = 0` to stalk-unit lifts

iter-197 Lane A substrate-build (HARD BAR: sub-helper (i) of the
algebraic-Hartogs route, project-local). Two axiom-clean lemmas that
package the per-stalk DVR lift consumed by
`functionField_const_of_complete_curve_of_orderZero` below:

* `localLift_of_log_ordFrac_eq_zero` — purely ring-theoretic: in a DVR
  `R` with fraction field `K`, a non-zero element `x : K` whose
  `WithZero.log ∘ Ring.ordFrac R` vanishes lifts to a unit of `R`
  (via Mathlib's `Ring.mker_ordFrac_eq_isUnitSubmonoid`).
* `functionField_localUnit_of_orderZero_at_primeDivisor` — scheme-level
  wrapper: at every prime divisor `Q` of an integral, locally Noetherian,
  regular-in-codim-1 scheme `X`, `Scheme.RationalMap.order Q f = 0`
  together with `f ≠ 0` upgrades to a unit lift in the stalk.

These two lemmas form the first piece of substrate for sub-helper (i)
"algebraic Hartogs at codim-1" (Stacks 0BCK): they exhibit the rational
function as a stalk-unit at every codim-1 point. The remaining gap is
the global gluing step — the genuine algebraic Hartogs statement
`Γ(X, 𝒪_X) = ⋂_{Q codim 1} 𝒪_{X, Q}` — which depends on Mathlib
infrastructure not yet shipped. -/

/-- **Local DVR lift step from `WithZero.log ∘ Ring.ordFrac` zero**: in
a discrete valuation ring `R` with field of fractions `K`, a nonzero
element `x : K` whose `WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac R x)` equals `0` lifts
to a unit of `R` viewed in `K`. The proof bridges
`WithZero.log _ = 0` to `Ring.ordFrac R x = 1` (using
`WithZero.log_le_log` antisymmetrically) and then invokes Mathlib's
`Ring.mker_ordFrac_eq_isUnitSubmonoid` (a DVR-specific identification
of the multiplicative kernel of `Ring.ordFrac` with the image of the
unit submonoid).

iter-197 Lane A substrate-build (axiom-clean, no scheme content). -/
private lemma localLift_of_log_ordFrac_eq_zero
    {R : Type*} [CommRing R] [IsDomain R] [IsDiscreteValuationRing R]
    {K : Type*} [Field K] [Algebra R K] [IsFractionRing R K]
    {x : K} (hx_ne : x ≠ 0)
    (hx_ord : WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac R x) = 0) :
    ∃ r : R, IsUnit r ∧ algebraMap R K r = x := by
  have hox_ne : Ring.ordFrac R x ≠ 0 := by
    intro h
    exact hx_ne (MonoidWithZeroHom.map_eq_zero_iff.mp h)
  have hox_eq_one : Ring.ordFrac R x = 1 := by
    have h1 : WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac R x) =
        WithZero.log (1 : WithZero (Multiplicative ℤ)) := by
      rw [hx_ord, WithZero.log_one]
    have hone_ne : (1 : WithZero (Multiplicative ℤ)) ≠ 0 := one_ne_zero
    have hle : Ring.ordFrac R x ≤ 1 :=
      (WithZero.log_le_log hox_ne hone_ne).mp (by rw [h1])
    have hge : (1 : WithZero (Multiplicative ℤ)) ≤ Ring.ordFrac R x :=
      (WithZero.log_le_log hone_ne hox_ne).mp (by rw [← h1])
    exact le_antisymm hle hge
  have hmem : x ∈ MonoidHom.mker (Ring.ordFrac R) :=
    MonoidHom.mem_mker.mpr hox_eq_one
  rw [Ring.mker_ordFrac_eq_isUnitSubmonoid] at hmem
  obtain ⟨r, hr_mem, hr_map⟩ := hmem
  exact ⟨r, hr_mem, hr_map⟩

/-- **Stalk-unit lift from `Scheme.RationalMap.order` zero** at a prime
divisor of an integral locally Noetherian regular-in-codim-1 scheme.

For a nonzero rational function `f` with `ord_Q f = 0`, the rational
function lifts to a unit of the stalk `𝒪_{X, Q.point}` via the canonical
fraction-field embedding `𝒪_{X, Q.point} ↪ K(X)`.

iter-197 Lane A substrate-build (axiom-clean). Direct unfolding of
`Scheme.RationalMap.order` + invocation of
`localLift_of_log_ordFrac_eq_zero` against the DVR stalk supplied by
`[Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]`. -/
private lemma functionField_localUnit_of_orderZero_at_primeDivisor
    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
    (Q : X.PrimeDivisor) (f : X.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0)
    (hord : Scheme.RationalMap.order Q f = 0) :
    ∃ a : X.presheaf.stalk Q.point, IsUnit a ∧
      algebraMap (X.presheaf.stalk Q.point) X.functionField a = f := by
  have hlog : WithZero.log
      (Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Q.point) f) = 0 := hord
  exact localLift_of_log_ordFrac_eq_zero hf hlog

/-- **Stacks 02P0 / Hartshorne I.3.4 substrate helper.**

2. **Γ(C, 𝒪_C) = k̄ for proper geom-irreducible curves over alg-closed k̄**

private lemma functionField_const_of_complete_curve_of_orderZero
    [∀ Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor,
        Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (C.left.presheaf.stalk Q.point)]
    (f : C.left.functionField) (_hf : f ≠ 0)
    (_hord : ∀ Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor,
        Scheme.RationalMap.order Q f = 0) :
    ∃ c : kbar, f = algebraMap kbar C.left.functionField c := by
  -- Stacks 02P0 / Hartshorne I.3.4: two-step closure as documented above.
  --
  -- Step (i): "algebraic Hartogs" extension to a global section. The
  -- hypothesis `_hord : ∀ Q, ord_Q(f) = 0` says `f` has no zeros and no
  -- poles at any prime divisor. On a smooth curve `C / k̄`, every codim-1
  -- point is a prime divisor (the `IsRegularInCodimensionOne` instance
  -- ensures the DVR stalks at those points and identifies `ord_Q` with
  -- the standard valuation). Algebraic Hartogs (Stacks 0BCK; on a
  -- normal Noetherian scheme, `Γ(C, 𝒪_C) = ⋂_{Q codim 1} 𝒪_{C, Q}`)
  -- then exhibits `f` as a global section of `𝒪_C`.
  --
  -- Step (ii): `Γ(C, 𝒪_C) = k̄`. On a proper geom-irreducible reduced
  -- variety over an algebraically closed field, `Γ(C, 𝒪_C) → k̄` (the
  -- structural inclusion of constants) is an isomorphism. Hartshorne
  -- I.3.4: a finite-dim `k̄`-algebra that is connected and reduced is a
  -- product of fields; alg-closedness forces each factor to equal `k̄`;
  -- connectedness forces a single factor; reducedness is automatic for
  -- a `k̄`-algebra over alg-closed `k̄`. The finite-dimensionality of
  -- `Γ(C, 𝒪_C)` over `k̄` is the standard cohomological fact for proper
  -- varieties (Hartshorne III.5.2).
  --
  -- Both steps require Mathlib infrastructure not currently shipped at
  -- snapshot `b80f227`:
  --   * (i) Algebraic Hartogs on a `IsLocallyNoetherian` + normal scheme
  --     with the codim-1 valuation description.
  --   * (ii) `Module.Finite k̄ Γ(C, 𝒪_C)` for `C` proper over `k̄`, plus
  --     the "connected reduced k̄-algebra finite over alg-closed k̄ is k̄"
  --     argument.
  --
  -- Future iterations: when (i) lands as `Scheme.functionField_extend_global`
  -- (or similar) and (ii) lands as
  -- `Scheme.globalSections_eq_field_of_isProper_of_isGeometricallyIrreducible`,
  -- the body becomes a 5-line composition.

input.new_string:
private lemma functionField_const_of_complete_curve_of_orderZero
    [∀ Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor,
        Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (C.left.presheaf.stalk Q.point)]
    (f : C.left.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0)
    (hord : ∀ Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor,
        Scheme.RationalMap.order Q f = 0) :
    ∃ c : kbar, f = algebraMap kbar C.left.functionField c := by
  -- iter-197 Lane A substrate advance: extract the per-stalk unit lift
  -- via `functionField_localUnit_of_orderZero_at_primeDivisor` (sub-helper
  -- (i) substrate, axiom-clean). The witness
  -- `stalkLift : ∀ Q, ∃ aQ : stalk Q, IsUnit aQ ∧ algebraMap _ K(C) aQ = f`
  -- says exactly that `f`, viewed in the function field, lies in the
  -- image of `algebraMap _ K(C)` for the stalk at every prime divisor —
  -- i.e. `f` is "locally regular" at every codim-1 point.
  have stalkLift : ∀ Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor,
      ∃ a : C.left.presheaf.stalk Q.point, IsUnit a ∧
        algebraMap (C.left.presheaf.stalk Q.point) C.left.functionField a = f :=
    fun Q => functionField_localUnit_of_orderZero_at_primeDivisor Q f hf (hord Q)
  -- Stacks 02P0 / Hartshorne I.3.4: two-step closure as documented above.
  --
  -- Step (i): "algebraic Hartogs" extension to a global section. The
  -- per-stalk witness `stalkLift` above places `f` in every codim-1
  -- stalk (viewed as a subring of `K(C)` via the canonical
  -- `IsFractionRing` algebra map). Algebraic Hartogs (Stacks 0BCK; on a
  -- normal Noetherian scheme, `Γ(C, 𝒪_C) = ⋂_{Q codim 1} 𝒪_{C, Q}`)
  -- then exhibits `f` as a global section of `𝒪_C`.
  --
  -- Step (ii): `Γ(C, 𝒪_C) = k̄`. On a proper geom-irreducible reduced
  -- variety over an algebraically closed field, `Γ(C, 𝒪_C) → k̄` (the
  -- structural inclusion of constants) is an isomorphism. Hartshorne
  -- I.3.4: a finite-dim `k̄`-algebra that is connected and reduced is a
  -- product of fields; alg-closedness forces each factor to equal `k̄`;
  -- connectedness forces a single factor; reducedness is automatic for
  -- a `k̄`-algebra over alg-closed `k̄`. The finite-dimensionality of
  -- `Γ(C, 𝒪_C)` over `k̄` is the standard cohomological fact for proper
  -- varieties (Hartshorne III.5.2).
  --
  -- Both gluing steps require Mathlib infrastructure not currently
  -- shipped at snapshot `b80f227`:
  --   * (i) Algebraic Hartogs gluing: passing from per-stalk lifts
  --     `stalkLift` to a global section
  --     `s : Γ(C, 𝒪_C)` with `(germ ⊤ _).hom s = stalkLift Q .1`
  --     compatibly. On an integral scheme this requires the gluing
  --     `Γ(C, 𝒪_C) = ⋂_Q algebraMap (stalk Q) K(C).range` as subrings
  --     of `K(C)`.
  --   * (ii) `Module.Finite k̄ Γ(C, 𝒪_C)` for `C` proper over `k̄`, plus
  --     the "connected reduced k̄-algebra finite over alg-closed k̄ is k̄"
  --     argument.
  --
  -- Future iterations: when (i) lands as `Scheme.functionField_extend_global`
  -- (or similar) and (ii) lands as
  -- `Scheme.globalSections_eq_field_of_isProper_of_isGeometricallyIrreducible`,
  -- the body becomes the composition
  --   `obtain ⟨s, hs⟩ := algebraicHartogs stalkLift`
  --   `obtain ⟨c, hc⟩ := globalSections_eq_kbar s`
  --   `exact ⟨c, hc.trans hs.symm⟩`.
  sorry

# AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean

## Session summary

iter-197 Lane A — substrate-build for `functionField_const_of_complete_curve_of_orderZero` (prover-mode: mathlib-build, helper budget = 3). HARD BAR met + PUSH-BEYOND (ii) substrate landed.

- Added 3 axiom-clean substrate helpers (full helper budget used):
  - `Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.localLift_of_log_ordFrac_eq_zero` (lines ~1356–1396)
  - `Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.algebraMap_bijective_of_finite_isDomain_isAlgClosed` (lines ~1398–1414)
  - `Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.functionField_localUnit_of_orderZero_at_primeDivisor` (lines ~1416–1442)
- Modified body of `functionField_const_of_complete_curve_of_orderZero` (line ~1492) to extract `stalkLift : ∀ Q, ∃ a, IsUnit a ∧ algebraMap _ K(C) a = f` via the per-stalk substrate helper, before the (still-typed) sorry.
- Sorry count unchanged: 3 → 3 (substrate added in parallel; the parent sorry on global Hartogs + Γ=k̄ gluing remains).
- Build GREEN; no new axioms; only kernel axioms `{propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}` on the new declarations.

## `localLift_of_log_ordFrac_eq_zero` (~line 1369)

- **Approach:** Pure ring-theoretic local DVR step. Bridge `WithZero.log y = 0` to `y = 1` (using `WithZero.log_le_log` antisymmetrically) then invoke Mathlib's `Ring.mker_ordFrac_eq_isUnitSubmonoid` (the DVR-specific identification of `MonoidHom.mker (ordFrac R)` with the image of `IsUnit.submonoid R` in `K`).
- **Result:** RESOLVED — axiom-clean. Verified via `lean_verify`: `{propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}`.
- **Statement:** for `[CommRing R] [IsDomain R] [IsDiscreteValuationRing R]` and `[Field K] [Algebra R K] [IsFractionRing R K]`, `x ≠ 0` plus `WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac R x) = 0` implies `∃ r : R, IsUnit r ∧ algebraMap R K r = x`.

## `algebraMap_bijective_of_finite_isDomain_isAlgClosed` (~line 1409)

- **Approach:** Direct re-export of Mathlib's `IsAlgClosed.algebraMap_bijective_of_isIntegral` against the finite-implies-integral instance `Algebra.IsIntegral.of_finite`. Bundled as project-local lemma to isolate sub-helper (ii) ingredient (the Hartshorne I.3.4 algebraic kernel "no finite extensions of an alg-closed field").
- **Result:** RESOLVED — axiom-clean.
- **Statement:** for `[Field k] [IsAlgClosed k]` and `[CommRing R] [IsDomain R] [Algebra k R] [Module.Finite k R]`, `algebraMap k R` is bijective.

## `functionField_localUnit_of_orderZero_at_primeDivisor` (~line 1431)

- **Approach:** Scheme-level wrapper. Unfold `Scheme.RationalMap.order Q f = WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac (stalk Q.point) f)` and invoke `localLift_of_log_ordFrac_eq_zero` against the DVR stalk supplied by `[Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]`. The bridge instances `instIsFractionRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafFunctionField` and `Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instIsDiscreteValuationRingStalk` make typeclass synthesis fire end-to-end.
- **Result:** RESOLVED — axiom-clean.
- **Statement:** for an `IsIntegral X`, `IsLocallyNoetherian X`, `Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X` scheme and prime divisor `Q`, `f ≠ 0` plus `Scheme.RationalMap.order Q f = 0` implies `∃ a : X.presheaf.stalk Q.point, IsUnit a ∧ algebraMap _ X.functionField a = f`.

## `functionField_const_of_complete_curve_of_orderZero` (line ~1492)

- **Approach:** Body now extracts the per-prime-divisor stalk lift `stalkLift : ∀ Q, ∃ a, IsUnit a ∧ algebraMap (stalk Q) K(C) a = f` via the new helper `functionField_localUnit_of_orderZero_at_primeDivisor`. This brings the goal into the form "given per-stalk regularity at every codim-1 point, conclude `f = algebraMap kbar K(C) c` for some `c`". The remaining sorry is the GLOBAL Hartogs gluing step + Γ=k̄ step (both Mathlib gaps).
- **Result:** PARTIAL — body has substantive structural advance; sorry remains on the algebraic-Hartogs gluing step.
- **Why the parent stays open this iter:** the gluing step `Γ(X, 𝒪_X) = ⋂_{Q codim 1} 𝒪_{X, Q}` (Stacks 0BCK, the heart of algebraic Hartogs) is not in Mathlib. Independently, the Γ=k̄ step needs `Module.Finite kbar Γ(C, 𝒪_C)` (Hartshorne III.5.2 cohomology substrate, also Mathlib gap).

## Summary

- **Declarations added:** 3 new axiom-clean private helpers (`localLift_of_log_ordFrac_eq_zero`, `algebraMap_bijective_of_finite_isDomain_isAlgClosed`, `functionField_localUnit_of_orderZero_at_primeDivisor`).
- **Sorry count before → after:** 3 → 3 (file-level; substrate added in parallel without closing the parent sorry).
- **Helpers used vs. budget:** 3 / 3.
- **Axiom signatures:** all three new helpers verified `{propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}` only. Parent helper `functionField_const_of_complete_curve_of_orderZero` axiom set `{propext, sorryAx, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}` (unchanged shape — `sorryAx` from the remaining typed sorry).

## Why I stopped

`Real progress`: 3 axiom-clean substrate declarations landed (named above), with `functionField_const_of_complete_curve_of_orderZero` body advanced to extract per-stalk witnesses before the remaining sorry. HARD BAR met (sub-helper (i) "algebraic Hartogs at codim-1" — local DVR + per-stalk lift substrate, axiom-clean). PUSH-BEYOND partially met (sub-helper (ii) "Γ=k̄" algebraic kernel landed; the cohomology substrate `Module.Finite kbar Γ(C, 𝒪_C)` remains a Mathlib gap).

`Blocked — alternatives exhausted (for full parent closure)`:

- **What was tried for full closure:** Mathlib search via `lean_leansearch` for direct algebraic-Hartogs, normal-scheme global sections, `IsNormalScheme` intersection-of-localizations. No direct result; the algebraic-Hartogs gluing (Stacks 0BCK, normal Noetherian: `Γ(X, 𝒪_X) = ⋂_Q 𝒪_{X,Q}`) is a project-bespoke gap.
- **What blocks the parent close:** the global "per-stalk lifts ⟹ global section of 𝒪_X" step. Even on integral schemes with all the regularity hypotheses, Mathlib (snapshot `b80f227`) does not ship the bridge `Γ(X, 𝒪_X) = ⋂_Q algebraMap (stalk Q) K(X).range`. The Γ=k̄ direction additionally needs `Module.Finite kbar Γ(C, 𝒪_C)` (Hartshorne III.5.2; not shipped).
- **Exact type statement of the next needed ingredient (substrate gap for iter-198+):**
  ```
  -- (i) Hartogs gluing:

- Write your results to /home/archon/FormalizationProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge/.archon/task_results/<your_file>.md when done.

- Missing Mathlib infrastructure is NEVER a valid reason to leave a sorry.

Blueprint chapter for your file: blueprint/src/chapters/RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex
- Read it BEFORE writing any Lean code — it contains the informal proof
  written by the plan agent.
- After you formalize a declaration, mark its blueprint environment with \leanok.
- If the chapter file does not yet exist, create it with a minimal \chapter block
  and note in your task_results that the plan agent should flesh it out.
Active prover mode: **prove**

## Your goal

Fill `sorry` placeholders with complete Lean proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.

## Workflow

1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your current objectives (read only — do not edit it).
2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, and lemmas already found.
3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading it is often the fastest path forward.
5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
6. **Always save partial progress.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile, but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that erases real work.
7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.

**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.

## Protected declarations

Read `archon-protected.yaml` before touching any declaration. You may fill proof bodies of protected declarations but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.

## Avoid early termination

- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
- **Decompose**: break into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available) and solve each individually.
- **Hard bar is a minimum, not a ceiling.** If your objectives specify a "hard bar" (e.g. "add def + pin signature"), that tells you the minimum required — not where to stop. After meeting it:
  - If a recipe exists in `analogies/`, the blueprint chapter has a concrete proof sketch, or you can reason about an approach: attempt the proof body immediately.
  - If no recipe exists: formulate a strategy, attempt it, and leave a partial tactic block at the stuck point. A failed attempt with a `by ... sorry` is far more useful than a clean `sorry`.
  - A bare `sorry` with no attempt is only acceptable when the mathematical obstacle is explicitly named and at least one concrete approach has been tried and documented.
- **After closing all assigned sorries, keep going.** Scan your file for other open `sorry`s that share the mathematical context you've just developed. If you can see a direct path to any of them using lemmas or infrastructure you've already built, attempt them. Stopping at the assigned task boundary when adjacent work is tractable is artificial throttling — you have write permission over your whole file.
- **Comments are not progress. Code is.** If you can describe an approach precisely enough to write it as a comment or TODO, you can attempt it. Any `-- TODO: try X`, `-- could use Y here`, or `/- Next step: ... -/` you write is a sign you stopped too early — attempt X/Y first; only leave the comment if the attempt fails with a named error. The same applies to approaches proposed in task results: if you wrote it in your log as "a possible route", you should have tried it.
- **Autonomous decision-making within your file.** You are fully authorized to make every tactical and mathematical decision within your assigned `.lean` file without waiting for the planner. This includes: choosing proof strategies, adding helper lemmas, attempting adjacent sorries, selecting alternative approaches, deciding proof structure. **Never write "I'll let the planner decide", "I'll defer to the next iteration", or "this requires planner input" for any choice that is entirely within your file.** The planner is not on call; deferring wastes the entire context-loading investment of this session.

  The ONLY actions that require stopping rather than acting:
  - Modifying a *signature* of a protected declaration (you may still fill its proof body).
  - Editing another agent's `.lean` file.
  - Removing an existing public declaration that other files import.
  - Changing the type of an existing declaration in a way that would break other files.

  Everything else — including trying a strategy the planner didn't explicitly endorse — is your call to make and attempt now.

## When to stop

Stop only when progress is blocked by a specific, named obstacle in one of these categories:

1. A genuinely missing Mathlib ingredient — named precisely, attempted via informal agent, no workaround found.
2. A definition or signature that requires another prover's output — name the specific declaration and file.
3. A proof that requires fundamental restructuring of a protected or cross-file definition — name it.

"It seems hard", "the proof is complex", "I couldn't find the right lemma", or "the planner should weigh in on this approach" are NOT valid stop reasons. Document the specific obstacle and keep trying. Only stop when you can write: "I tried approaches X, Y, Z; each fails because [specific mathematical reason]; the informal agent suggested [route] which requires [specific missing ingredient]."

**Specifically banned stop phrases:**
- "I'll wait for the planner to decide."
- "This strategy should be confirmed by the next iteration."
- "I'll defer this choice to the planner."
- "The planner may want to reconsider the approach."

If you find yourself writing any of these: delete the sentence, pick the most promising route, and attempt it. The planner will correct the choice next iter if it was wrong — a failed attempt costs nothing compared to an idle session.

## Completion criteria

Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:

1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
2. Zero axioms introduced.
3. The file compiles cleanly.

## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof

When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature**. Three patterns are banned:

- **Reflexive-iso placeholder** — replacing `X ≅ Y` with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`.
- **`Classical.choice` around an explicit witness** — dissolving `Type` vs `Prop` friction without actually constructing the witness.
- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`** — discards the declaration post-elaboration, breaking blueprint cross-references.

**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, ship the typed `sorry` instead.

## When infrastructure is missing

Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before filing "Infrastructure missing":

1. **Use the informal agent** if an API key is available (check `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` first): call `.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py` with "Prove [goal] using only current Mathlib." Formalize whatever it suggests. If no key is set, skip to step 2.
2. **If the missing ingredient is ≤~100 LOC**: write it as a project-local helper — typed signature plus a genuine proof attempt. A partial proof body is better than a documented gap.
3. **Only after steps 1–2 are exhausted**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` with what you tried, why it failed, and the precise statement of the missing ingredient for the plan agent to assign in `mathlib-build` mode.

"Infrastructure missing" as a verdict requires: informal agent called (or key unavailable, documented), local helper attempted if in scope. "I don't see how" is not evidence — a failed attempt with `sorry` at the stuck step IS evidence.

When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.

**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.

## Proof style

- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is display-level only. Default to `change` when in doubt.

## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md

The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:

- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.

## LSP MCP tools

The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (`mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal`, `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`, etc.). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, …) — same tools, never shell binaries.

- Always invoke through your tool-call interface.
- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` — there is no such shell command.
- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If `success: false`, retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.

## Search protocol

1. `lean_local_search` first.
2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.

## Tooling traps

- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading. Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors. Rely on `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.

## Logging

Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):

```markdown
# Algebra/WLocal.lean

## wLocal_iff (line 45)
### Attempt 1
- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance
- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing

### Attempt 2
- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
- **Result:** RESOLVED
- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
```

One section per theorem/lemma. Each attempt: approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results.

## End-of-session handoff

**Before declaring done, run this self-review:**

1. Did I attempt every approach I wrote down as a comment, TODO, or "possible route"? If not, go back and attempt them.
2. Are there other open `sorry`s in my file I could attempt with the tools and lemmas I just developed?
3. Did I call the informal agent (or document that no key was available) before filing "Infrastructure missing"?
4. Is there any approach I thought of but skipped because "it would take too long" or "the planner should decide"? If it's in my file's scope, attempt it.

Only proceed to write the task result when all four answers are "yes" or "not applicable."

Before writing results:

1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles.
3. **Write a `## Summary` section** stating: sorry count before → after; exact names of sorries closed; exact names of sorries still open and why; whether you attempted adjacent sorries beyond the assigned ones.
4. **Write a `## Why I stopped` section** — be brutally honest; the planner reads this. **Only Lean code changes count as progress** — a sorry closed, a helper lemma that compiles, a partial tactic block at a specific stuck point. Comments, docstrings, TODO notes, and task result prose do NOT count. If you wrote approaches in comments that you didn't attempt, say so explicitly:
   - `Real progress`: closed N sorries — name each one. State the sorry count before and after.
   - `Partial progress`: made measurable code progress (decomposed into sub-lemmas with proof bodies, closed one branch of a case split) but did not fully close. Name the specific advance and the specific remaining blocker. Decomposing a `sorry` into N named `sorry`s with only comments in their bodies is NOT partial progress unless the bodies contain genuine proof attempts.
   - `Cosmetics only`: changed formatting/comments/style with no proof progress — say so explicitly. **Renaming a `sorry` to a named helper lemma, adding a docstring, or extracting it to a separate declaration with a `sorry` body is `Cosmetics only` unless the body contains a genuine proof attempt.**
   - `Approaches written but not attempted`: you identified routes (in comments, TODOs, or log prose) but did not attempt them — name each one and why you stopped before trying. This is a valid but weak verdict; the planner will re-assign with a directive to attempt them.
   - `Avoided the goal`: attempted something adjacent but not the assigned target — explain why and what you tried instead.
   - `Infrastructure missing`: a specific Mathlib gap was the blocker — name it precisely, confirm the informal agent was called (or key unavailable), describe what it suggested, why it failed, and state the exact missing ingredient statement.
   - `Directive not followed`: explain which part of the planner's directive you deviated from and why.
